infobox.value: is a [city](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom")[[a]](#cite_note-1) in [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire"), England, situated 29 miles (47 km) south of [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") and 32 miles (51 km) east of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"). The city is the administrative centre of the [City of Sheffield](/wiki/City_of_Sheffield "City of Sheffield"). It is [historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") part of the [West Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire"). It is the largest settlement in South Yorkshire,[[1]](#cite_note-2)[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4) and the third largest in [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England").[[4]](#cite_note-ONS_release_August_2023-5)

**Sheffield** is a [city](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom")[[a]](#cite_note-1) in [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire"), England, situated 29 miles (47 km) south of [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") and 32 miles (51 km) east of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"). The city is the administrative centre of the [City of Sheffield](/wiki/City_of_Sheffield "City of Sheffield"). It is [historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") part of the [West Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire"). It is the largest settlement in South Yorkshire,[[1]](#cite_note-2)[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4) and the third largest in [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England").[[4]](#cite_note-ONS_release_August_2023-5)

The city is in the eastern foothills of the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") and the valleys of the [River Don](/wiki/River_Don,_Yorkshire "River Don, Yorkshire") with its four tributaries: the [Loxley](/wiki/River_Loxley "River Loxley"), the [Porter Brook](/wiki/Porter_Brook "Porter Brook"), the [Rivelin](/wiki/River_Rivelin "River Rivelin") and the [Sheaf](/wiki/River_Sheaf "River Sheaf"). Sixty-one per cent of Sheffield's entire area is green space and a third of the city lies within the [Peak District](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District") [national park](/wiki/National_park "National park") and it is the fifth-largest city in England.[[5]](#cite_note-sheffgeog-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) There are more than 250 parks, woodlands and gardens in the city,[[5]](#cite_note-sheffgeog-6) which is estimated to contain around 4.5 million trees.[[7]](#cite_note-GEO_treecount-8)

Sheffield played a crucial role in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), developing many significant technologies. In the 19th century, the city saw a huge expansion of its traditional cutlery trade, when processes for high-quality [stainless steel](/wiki/Stainless_steel "Stainless steel") and [crucible steel](/wiki/Crucible_steel "Crucible steel") were invented. This fuelled an almost tenfold increase in the population. Sheffield received its [municipal charter](/wiki/Municipal_charter "Municipal charter") in 1843, becoming the City of Sheffield in 1893. International competition in iron and steel caused a decline in these industries in the 1970s and 1980s, coinciding with the collapse of coal mining in the area. The Yorkshire [ridings](/wiki/Riding_(division) "Riding (division)") became counties in their own right in 1889; the [West Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire") county was disbanded in 1974. The city then became part of the county of [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire"); this has been made up of separately governed [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities "Unitary authorities") since 1986. The 21st century has seen extensive [redevelopment](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") in Sheffield, consistent with other British cities. Sheffield's [gross value added](/wiki/Gross_value_added "Gross value added") (GVA) has increased by 60% since 1997, standing at £11.3 billion in 2015. The economy has experienced steady growth, averaging around 5% annually, which is greater than that of the broader region of Yorkshire and the Humber.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Sheffield had a population of 556,500 at the 2021 census, making it the second-largest city in the [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber "Yorkshire and the Humber") region. The [Sheffield Built-up Area](/wiki/Sheffield_Urban_Area "Sheffield Urban Area"), of which the Sheffield sub-division is the largest part, had a population of 685,369 also including the town of [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham"). The [district borough](/wiki/City_of_Sheffield "City of Sheffield"), governed from the city, had a population of 582,493 at the mid-2019 estimate, making it the [7th](/wiki/List_of_English_districts_by_population "List of English districts by population")-most-populous district in England. It is one of eleven British cities that make up the [Core Cities Group](/wiki/Core_Cities_Group "Core Cities Group").[[9]](#cite_note-10) In 2011, the unparished area had a population of 490,070.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The city has a long sporting heritage and is home both to the world's oldest football club, [Sheffield F.C.](/wiki/Sheffield_F.C. "Sheffield F.C."),[[11]](#cite_note-celebration-12) and the world's oldest football ground, [Sandygate](/wiki/Sandygate_(stadium) "Sandygate (stadium)").[[12]](#cite_note-13) Matches between the two professional clubs, [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") and [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."), are known as the [Steel City derby](/wiki/Steel_City_derby "Steel City derby"). The city is also home to the [World Snooker Championship](/wiki/World_Snooker_Championship "World Snooker Championship") and the [Sheffield Steelers](/wiki/Sheffield_Steelers "Sheffield Steelers"), the UK's first professional ice hockey team.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

Sheffield takes its name from the [River Sheaf](/wiki/River_Sheaf "River Sheaf"), which runs through the city centre (albeit in underground [culverts](/wiki/Culvert "Culvert")).[[13]](#cite_note-14) The river's name comes from the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") word *scēað*, meaning "boundary", possibly because the Sheaf once formed part of the boundary between the kingdoms of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") and [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria").[[14]](#cite_note-15) The second element is Old English *feld*, meaning "open countryside", and the name thus means "open countryside near the River Sheaf". It first appears in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book").[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Sheffield](/wiki/History_of_Sheffield "History of Sheffield")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![Lithograph drawing showing a large stately home in ruins](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ruins_of_Sheffield_Manor_1819_1.jpg/250px-Ruins_of_Sheffield_Manor_1819_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruins_of_Sheffield_Manor_1819_1.jpg)

[Sheffield Manor](/wiki/Sheffield_Manor "Sheffield Manor") ruins as they appeared c. 1819

The area now occupied by the City of Sheffield is believed to have been inhabited since at least the late [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic"), about 12,800 years ago.[[16]](#cite_note-17) The earliest evidence of human occupation in the Sheffield area was found at [Creswell Crags](/wiki/Creswell_Crags "Creswell Crags") to the east of the city. In the [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age") the area became the southernmost territory of the [Pennine](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") tribe called the [Brigantes](/wiki/Brigantes "Brigantes"). It is this tribe who are thought to have constructed several [hillforts](/wiki/Hillfort "Hillfort") in and around Sheffield.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18)

Following the departure of the Romans, the Sheffield area may have been the southern part of the [Brittonic](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") kingdom of [Elmet](/wiki/Elmet "Elmet"), with the rivers Sheaf and Don forming part of the boundary between this kingdom and the kingdom of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia").[[18]](#cite_note-19) Gradually, [Anglian](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") settlers pushed west from the kingdom of [Deira](/wiki/Deira "Deira"). A Britonnic presence within the Sheffield area is evidenced by two settlements called [Wales](/wiki/Wales,_South_Yorkshire "Wales, South Yorkshire") and Waleswood close to Sheffield.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The settlements that grew and merged to form Sheffield, however, date from the second half of the first millennium, and are of [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") and [Danish](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") origin.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18) In Anglo-Saxon times, the Sheffield area straddled the border between the kingdoms of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") and [Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"). The *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* reports that [Eanred of Northumbria](/wiki/Eanred_of_Northumbria "Eanred of Northumbria") submitted to [Egbert of Wessex](/wiki/Egbert_of_Wessex "Egbert of Wessex") at the hamlet of [Dore](/wiki/Dore,_South_Yorkshire "Dore, South Yorkshire") (now a suburb of south west Sheffield) in 829,[[20]](#cite_note-21) a key event in the unification of the kingdom of England under the [House of Wessex](/wiki/House_of_Wessex "House of Wessex").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

After the [Norman conquest of England](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England"), insurrection by the populace led to the devastation of the Sheffield area during the [Harrying of the North](/wiki/Harrying_of_the_North "Harrying of the North")[[22]](#cite_note-23) and [Sheffield Castle](/wiki/Sheffield_Castle "Sheffield Castle") was built to control the ruined local settlements. A small town developed that is the nucleus of the modern city.[[23]](#cite_note-24) By 1296, a market had been established at what is now known as [Castle Square](/wiki/Castle_Square,_Sheffield "Castle Square, Sheffield"),[[24]](#cite_note-25) and Sheffield subsequently grew into a small [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town"). In the 14th century, Sheffield was already noted for the production of [knives](/wiki/Knives "Knives"), as mentioned in [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")'s *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*,[[25]](#cite_note-26) and by the early 1600s it had become the main centre of [cutlery](/wiki/Cutlery "Cutlery") manufacture in England outside London, overseen by the [Company of Cutlers in Hallamshire](/wiki/Company_of_Cutlers_in_Hallamshire "Company of Cutlers in Hallamshire").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

From 1570 to 1584, [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots"), was imprisoned in Sheffield Castle and [Sheffield Manor](/wiki/Sheffield_Manor "Sheffield Manor") by [George Talbot, 6th Earl of Shrewsbury](/wiki/George_Talbot,_6th_Earl_of_Shrewsbury "George Talbot, 6th Earl of Shrewsbury"), the husband of [Bess of Hardwick, Countess of Shrewsbury](/wiki/Bess_of_Hardwick "Bess of Hardwick").[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Earl is buried in Sheffield Cathedral.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Industrial Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Industrial Revolution")]

[![19th century picture of Sheffield](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/%27Owd_Shevvield%27.jpg/250px-%27Owd_Shevvield%27.jpg)](/wiki/File:%27Owd_Shevvield%27.jpg)

Sheffield in the 19th century. The dominance of industry in the city is evident.

[![Bombing in Sheffield during the Sheffield Blitz, WW2.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/13th_December_1940-_Sheffield_blitz_-_NARA_-_196508.jpg/250px-13th_December_1940-_Sheffield_blitz_-_NARA_-_196508.jpg)](/wiki/File:13th_December_1940-_Sheffield_blitz_-_NARA_-_196508.jpg)

Sheffield was targeted heavily by the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") during WW2, owing to the city's industrial importance. The bombing campaign became known as the [Sheffield Blitz](/wiki/Sheffield_Blitz "Sheffield Blitz").

During the 1740s, a form of the [crucible steel](/wiki/Crucible_steel "Crucible steel") process was discovered that allowed the manufacture of a better quality of steel than had previously been possible.[[29]](#cite_note-Tweedale1986-30) In about the same period, a technique was developed for fusing a thin sheet of silver onto a copper ingot to produce silver plating, which became widely known as [Sheffield plate](/wiki/Sheffield_plate "Sheffield plate").[[30]](#cite_note-31) These innovations spurred Sheffield's growth as an industrial town,[[31]](#cite_note-32) but the loss of some important export markets led to a recession in the late 18th and early 19th centuries. The resulting poor conditions culminated in a [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera") epidemic that killed 402 people in 1832.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18) The population of the town grew rapidly throughout the 19th century; increasing from 60,095 in 1801 to 451,195 by 1901.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18) The [Sheffield and Rotherham railway](/wiki/Sheffield_and_Rotherham_Railway "Sheffield and Rotherham Railway") was constructed in 1838, connecting the two towns. The town was incorporated as a [borough](/wiki/Borough "Borough") in 1842, and was granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") by [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") in 1893.[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The influx of people also led to demand for better water supplies, and a number of new [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir") were constructed on the hills to the west of the town.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The collapse of the dam wall of one of these reservoirs in 1864 resulted in the [Great Sheffield Flood](/wiki/Great_Sheffield_Flood "Great Sheffield Flood"), which killed 270 people and devastated large parts of the town.[[35]](#cite_note-36) In 1880 ten men from Sheffield were part of a group of 47 men who had attended a fancy-dress ball in Hulme, Manchester, but they were tried for soliciting sex between men.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-37) Described by historian Lauren Wells as the 'Sheffield Ten', men from the city had their addresses published in local news.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-37)

The growing population led to the construction of many back-to-back dwellings that, along with severe pollution from the factories, inspired [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell") in 1937 to write: "Sheffield, I suppose, could justly claim to be called the ugliest town in the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World")".[[37]](#cite_note-38)

[![Photo of the statue Women of Steel at barker's Pool, Sheffield](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Women_of_steel.jpg/250px-Women_of_steel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Women_of_steel.jpg)

The *[Women of Steel](/wiki/Women_of_Steel "Women of Steel")* statue commemorates the women of Sheffield who worked in the city's steel industry during the First and Second World Wars.

### Blitz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Blitz")]

Main article: [Sheffield Blitz](/wiki/Sheffield_Blitz "Sheffield Blitz")

[The Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") hit the city in the 1930s, but as international tensions increased and the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") became imminent, Sheffield's steel factories were set to work manufacturing weapons and ammunition for the war effort. As a result, the city became a target for bombing raids, the heaviest of which occurred on the nights of 12 and 15 December 1940, now known as the [Sheffield Blitz](/wiki/Sheffield_Blitz "Sheffield Blitz"). The city was partially protected by [barrage balloons](/wiki/Barrage_balloon "Barrage balloon") managed from [RAF Norton](/wiki/RAF_Norton "RAF Norton").[[38]](#cite_note-39) More than 660 people died and many buildings were destroyed or left badly damaged, including the Marples Hotel, which was hit directly by a 500-pound bomb, killing over 70 people.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### Post-Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-Second World War")]

See also: [Brutalism in Sheffield](/wiki/Brutalism_in_Sheffield "Brutalism in Sheffield")

[![Panorama of a brutalist housing estate](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Parkhill2.jpg/250px-Parkhill2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parkhill2.jpg)

[Park Hill flats](/wiki/Park_Hill,_Sheffield "Park Hill, Sheffield"), an example of 1950s and 1960s council housing estates in Sheffield

In the 1950s and 1960s, many of the city's slums were demolished, and replaced with housing schemes such as the [Park Hill flats](/wiki/Park_Hill,_Sheffield "Park Hill, Sheffield"). Large parts of the city centre were also cleared to make way for a new system of roads.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18) In February 1962, the city was devastated by the [Great Sheffield Gale](/wiki/Great_Sheffield_Gale "Great Sheffield Gale"); winds of up to 97 mph (156 km/h) killed four people and damaged 150,000 houses, more than two-thirds of the city's housing stock at the time.[[40]](#cite_note-rmets-41) Increased automation and competition from abroad resulted in the [closure of many steel mills](/wiki/Steel_crisis "Steel crisis"). The 1980s saw the worst of this run-down of Sheffield's industries, along with those of many other areas of the UK.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The building of the [Meadowhall Centre](/wiki/Meadowhall_Centre "Meadowhall Centre") on the site of a former steelworks in 1990 was a mixed blessing, creating much-needed jobs but hastening the decline of the city centre. Attempts to regenerate the city were kick-started when the city hosted the 1991 [World Student Games](/wiki/Universiade "Universiade"), which saw the construction of new sporting facilities such as the [Sheffield Arena](/wiki/Sheffield_Arena "Sheffield Arena"), [Don Valley Stadium](/wiki/Don_Valley_Stadium "Don Valley Stadium") and the [Ponds Forge](/wiki/Ponds_Forge "Ponds Forge") complex.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 21st century")]

Sheffield is changing rapidly as new projects regenerate some of the more run-down parts of the city. One such, the *Heart of the City Project*, with £470 million investment,[[42]](#cite_note-43) has initiated a number of public works in the city centre: the [Peace Gardens](/wiki/Peace_Gardens "Peace Gardens") were renovated in 1998, the [Millennium Gallery](/wiki/Millennium_Galleries "Millennium Galleries") opened in April 2001, the [Winter Gardens](/wiki/Sheffield_Winter_Gardens "Sheffield Winter Gardens") were opened in May 2003, and a public space to link these two areas, the [Millennium Square](/wiki/Millennium_Square_(Sheffield) "Millennium Square (Sheffield)"), was opened in May 2006. Additional developments included the remodelling of [Sheaf Square](/wiki/Sheaf_Square "Sheaf Square"), in front of the refurbished railway station: the square contains "The Cutting Edge", a sculpture designed by Si Applied Ltd[[43]](#cite_note-44) and made from Sheffield steel. Recent development known as the 'Heart of the City' includes the restoration of the listed Leah's Yard complex.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Sheffield was particularly hard hit during the [2007 United Kingdom floods](/wiki/2007_United_Kingdom_floods "2007 United Kingdom floods") and the [2010 'Big Freeze'](/wiki/Winter_of_2009%E2%80%9310_in_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Winter of 2009–10 in Great Britain and Ireland").[[45]](#cite_note-46) Many landmark buildings such as Meadowhall and the [Hillsborough Stadium](/wiki/Hillsborough_Stadium "Hillsborough Stadium") flooded due to being close to rivers that flow through the city.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In 2010, 5,000 properties in Sheffield were identified as still being at risk of flooding. In 2012 the city narrowly escaped another flood, despite extensive work by the Environment Agency to clear local river channels since the 2007 event. In 2014 Sheffield Council's cabinet approved plans to further reduce the possibility of flooding by adopting plans to increase water catchment on tributaries of the River Don.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) [Another flood hit the city in 2019](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_United_Kingdom_floods#Sheffield "2019–20 United Kingdom floods"), resulting in shoppers being contained in Meadowhall Shopping Centre.[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Between 2014 and 2018, there were [disputes](/wiki/Sheffield_tree_felling_protests "Sheffield tree felling protests") between the city council and residents over the fate of the city's 36,000 highway trees. Around 4,000 highway trees have since been felled as part of the 'Streets Ahead' [Private Finance Initiative](/wiki/Private_Finance_Initiative "Private Finance Initiative") (PFI) contract signed in 2012 by the city council, [Amey plc](/wiki/Amey_plc "Amey plc") and the [Department for Transport](/wiki/Department_for_Transport "Department for Transport") to maintain the city streets.[[52]](#cite_note-53) The tree fellings have resulted in many arrests of residents and other protesters across the city even though most felled trees in the city have been replanted, including those historically felled and not previously replanted.[[53]](#cite_note-54) The protests eventually stopped in 2018 after the council paused the tree felling programme as part of a new approach developed by the council for the maintenance of street trees in the city.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In May 2022, Sheffield was named a "[Tree City of the World](/wiki/Tree_Cities_of_the_World "Tree Cities of the World")" in recognition of its work to sustainably manage and maintain urban forests and trees.[[55]](#cite_note-56) This honour was given before the release of the independent inquiry's report on the so-called "Sheffield Chainsaw Massacre". The report concluded that "thousands of healthy and loved trees were lost. Many more could have been" and was critical of Sheffield City Council. The latter issued this statement on receipt of the report: "the council has already acknowledged that it got many things wrong in the handling of the street-trees dispute, and we wish to reiterate our previous apologies for our failings".[[56]](#cite_note-57)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governance")]

See also: [Sheffield City Council elections](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council_elections "Sheffield City Council elections")

### Local authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Local authority")]

[![Sheffield Town Hall](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Sheffield-town-hall-staircase.jpg/250px-Sheffield-town-hall-staircase.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield-town-hall-staircase.jpg)

The staircase at Sheffield Town Hall

Sheffield is governed at the local level by [Sheffield City Council](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council "Sheffield City Council") and is led by Councillor Tom Hunt (Assumed office 16 May 2023).[[57]](#cite_note-58) It consists of 84 councillors elected to represent 28 [wards](/wiki/Ward_(electoral_subdivision) "Ward (electoral subdivision)"): three councillors per ward. Following the [2024 local elections](/wiki/2024_Sheffield_City_Council_election "2024 Sheffield City Council election"), the distribution of council seats is [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") 27, [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") 21, [Labour and Co-operative Party](/wiki/Labour_and_Co-operative_Party "Labour and Co-operative Party") 15, the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") 14, Sheffield Community Councillors Group 4, [Independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") 2, and [Reform](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") 1.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The city also has a [Lord Mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Sheffield "List of mayors of Sheffield"); though now simply a ceremonial position, in the past the office carried considerable authority, with executive powers over the finances and affairs of the city council. The position of Lord Mayor is elected on an annual basis.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

For much of its history the council was controlled by the Labour Party, and was noted for its [leftist](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") sympathies; during the 1980s, when Sheffield City Council was led by [David Blunkett](/wiki/David_Blunkett "David Blunkett"), the area gained the epithet the "[Socialist Republic of South Yorkshire](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_South_Yorkshire "People's Republic of South Yorkshire")".[[60]](#cite_note-61) However, the Liberal Democrats controlled the Council between 1999 and 2002,[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) and took control again from 2008 to 2011.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65)

The majority of council-owned facilities are operated by independent charitable trusts. [Sheffield International Venues](/wiki/Sheffield_International_Venues "Sheffield International Venues") runs many of the city's sporting and leisure facilities, including [Sheffield Arena](/wiki/Sheffield_Arena "Sheffield Arena") and the [English Institute of Sport, Sheffield](/wiki/English_Institute_of_Sport,_Sheffield "English Institute of Sport, Sheffield"). [Sheffield Museums](/wiki/Sheffield_Museums_Trust "Sheffield Museums Trust") take care of galleries and museums owned by the council.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### Combined authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Combined authority")]

Main article: [South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Mayoral_Combined_Authority "South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority")

The city of Sheffield is part of the wider [South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Mayoral_Combined_Authority "South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority"), headed by mayor [Oliver Coppard](/wiki/Oliver_Coppard "Oliver Coppard") since 2022.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The [combined authority](/wiki/Combined_authority "Combined authority") covers the former 1974–1986 [South Yorkshire County Council](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_County_Council "South Yorkshire County Council") area which functions either went to local or regional authorities.

In 2004, as part of the *Moving Forward: [The Northern Way](/wiki/The_Northern_Way "The Northern Way")* document,[[68]](#cite_note-Moving_Forward:The_Northern_Way-69) city regions were created in a collaboration with the three northern [regional development agencies](/wiki/Regional_development_agency "Regional development agency"). These became independent [Local enterprise partnerships](/wiki/Local_enterprise_partnership "Local enterprise partnership") in 2011.

The area's partnership retains the Sheffield City Region name, covering the South Yorkshire authorities, as well as [Bolsover District](/wiki/Bolsover_District "Bolsover District"), [Borough of Chesterfield](/wiki/Borough_of_Chesterfield "Borough of Chesterfield"), [Derbyshire Dales](/wiki/Derbyshire_Dales "Derbyshire Dales"), [North East Derbyshire](/wiki/North_East_Derbyshire "North East Derbyshire") and [Bassetlaw District](/wiki/Bassetlaw_District "Bassetlaw District"). In 2014, the *Sheffield City Region Combined authority* was formed by the South Yorkshire [local authorities](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") with the other councils as non-constituent members and the partnership integrated with the authority structure. In September 2020, the authority changed to its current name.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### Parliamentary Representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Parliamentary Representation")]

The city returns five members of parliament to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), with a sixth, the Member of Parliament for [Penistone and Stocksbridge](/wiki/Penistone_and_Stocksbridge "Penistone and Stocksbridge") representing parts of Sheffield and Barnsley.[[70]](#cite_note-71) The former Deputy Prime Minister [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") was an MP for Sheffield, representing [Sheffield Hallam](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Hallam (UK Parliament constituency)") from [2005](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") until he was unseated [2017](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), when the seat returned a Labour MP for the first time in its history.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Sheffield](/wiki/Geography_of_Sheffield "Geography of Sheffield")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Sheffield_City_Centre_Panorama_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1930479.jpg/960px-Sheffield_City_Centre_Panorama_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1930479.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_City_Centre_Panorama_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1930479.jpg)

Sheffield panorama

Sheffield is located at [53°22′59″N 1°27′57″W﻿ / ﻿53.38297°N 1.4659°W﻿ / 53.38297; -1.4659](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Sheffield&params=53.38297_N_1.4659_W_region:GB-SHF_type:city_source:wikidata). It lies directly beside [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham"), from which it is separated largely by the [M1 motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway"). Although [Barnsley Metropolitan Borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Barnsley "Metropolitan Borough of Barnsley") also borders Sheffield to the north, the town itself is a few miles further away. The southern and western borders of the city are shared with [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire"); in the first half of the 20th century Sheffield extended its borders south into Derbyshire, annexing a number of villages,[[72]](#cite_note-derbyshire_expansion-73) including [Totley](/wiki/Totley "Totley"), [Dore](/wiki/Dore,_South_Yorkshire "Dore, South Yorkshire") and the area now known as [Mosborough](/wiki/Mosborough "Mosborough") Townships.

[![Rivelin Valley](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Rivelin_Valley_-_geograph.org.uk_-_171316.jpg/250px-Rivelin_Valley_-_geograph.org.uk_-_171316.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rivelin_Valley_-_geograph.org.uk_-_171316.jpg)

[Rivelin Valley](/wiki/Rivelin_Valley "Rivelin Valley")

Sheffield is a geographically diverse city.[[73]](#cite_note-GEO_diverse-74) It nestles in the eastern foothills of the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines"),[[74]](#cite_note-75) between the main upland range and [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District") to the west, and the lower-lying [South Yorkshire Coalfield](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Coalfield "South Yorkshire Coalfield") to the east. It lies at the confluence of five rivers: [Don](/wiki/River_Don,_South_Yorkshire "River Don, South Yorkshire"), [Sheaf](/wiki/River_Sheaf "River Sheaf"), [Rivelin](/wiki/River_Rivelin "River Rivelin"), [Loxley](/wiki/River_Loxley "River Loxley") and [Porter](/wiki/Porter_Brook "Porter Brook"). As such, much of the city is built on hillsides with views into the city centre or out to the countryside. Blake Street, in the S6 postcode area, is the third-steepest residential street in England, with a gradient of 16.6°.[[75]](#cite_note-76) The highest point in the City of Sheffield is 548 m (1,798 ft) near [High Stones](/wiki/High_Stones "High Stones") and [Margery Hill](/wiki/Margery_Hill "Margery Hill").[[76]](#cite_note-77) The city's lowest point is just 29 m (95 ft) above sea level near [Blackburn Meadows](/wiki/Blackburn_Meadows "Blackburn Meadows"). However, 79% of the housing in the city is between 100 and 200 m (330 and 660 ft) above sea level[[77]](#cite_note-78) and the highest residential street is Redmires Lane at 302 m (991 ft).[[78]](#cite_note-79) This variation of altitudes across Sheffield has led to frequent claims, particularly among locals, that the city was built on Seven Hills. As this claim is disputed, it likely originated as a joke referencing the [Seven Hills of Rome](/wiki/Seven_Hills_of_Rome "Seven Hills of Rome").[[79]](#cite_note-number_of_hills-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)

[![Rivelin Valley](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Gleadless_Valley_view.jpg/250px-Gleadless_Valley_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gleadless_Valley_view.jpg)

[Gleadless](/wiki/Gleadless "Gleadless") Valley, demonstrating the hilly terrain within the city

Estimated to contain around 4.5 million trees,[[7]](#cite_note-GEO_treecount-8) Sheffield has more trees per person than any other city in Europe and is considered to be one of the greenest cities in England and the UK,[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-The_four_best_UK_cities_for_green_space-83) which was further reinforced when it won the 2005 [Entente Florale](/wiki/Entente_Florale "Entente Florale") competition. With more than 250 parks, woodlands and gardens, it has over 170 woodlands (covering 10.91 mi2 or 28.3 km2), 78 public parks (covering 7.07 mi2 or 18.3 km2) and 10 public gardens. Added to the 52.0 mi2 (134.7 km2) of national park and 4.20 mi2 (10.9 km2) of water this means that 61% of the city is [greenspace](/wiki/Open_space_reserve "Open space reserve"). Despite this, about 64% of Sheffield householders live further than 300 m (328 yd) from their nearest greenspace, although access is better in less affluent neighbourhoods across the city.[[5]](#cite_note-sheffgeog-6)[[83]](#cite_note-84) Sheffield also has a very wide variety of [habitat](/wiki/Habitat "Habitat"), comparing favourably with any city in the United Kingdom: urban, parkland and woodland, agricultural and arable land, moors, meadows and freshwater-based habitats. There are six areas within the city that are designated as [sites of special scientific interest](/wiki/List_of_Sites_of_Special_Scientific_Interest_in_South_Yorkshire "List of Sites of Special Scientific Interest in South Yorkshire").[[84]](#cite_note-85)

The present city boundaries were set in 1974 (with slight modification in 1994), when the former [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") of Sheffield merged with [Stocksbridge](/wiki/Stocksbridge "Stocksbridge") [Urban District](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") and two parishes from the [Wortley Rural District](/wiki/Wortley_Rural_District "Wortley Rural District").[[5]](#cite_note-sheffgeog-6) This area includes a significant part of the countryside surrounding the main urban region. Roughly a third of Sheffield lies in the [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District"). No other English city had parts of a national park within its boundary,[[85]](#cite_note-86) until the creation in March 2010 of the [South Downs National Park](/wiki/South_Downs_National_Park "South Downs National Park"), part of which lies within [Brighton and Hove](/wiki/Brighton_and_Hove "Brighton and Hove").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Sheffield has a [temperate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate") [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (*Cfb*) like the rest of the United Kingdom. The uplands of the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") to the west can create a cool, gloomy and wet environment, but they also provide shelter from the prevailing westerly winds, casting a "[rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow")" across the area.[[86]](#cite_note-Met_NE_England-87) Between 1991 and 2020 Sheffield averaged 831.6 mm (32.74 in) of rain per year; December was the wettest month with 86.9 mm (3.42 in) and May the driest with 54.5 mm (2.15 in). July was the hottest month, with an average maximum temperature of 21.4 °C (70.5 °F). The highest temperature ever recorded in the city of Sheffield was 39.4 °C (102.9 °F), on 19 July 2022.[[87]](#cite_note-88) The average minimum temperature in January and February was 2.2 °C (36.0 °F),[[88]](#cite_note-89) though the lowest temperatures recorded in these months can be between −10 and −15 °C (14 and 5 °F), although since 1960, the temperature has never fallen below −9.2 °C (15.4 °F),[[89]](#cite_note-90) suggesting that urbanisation around the Weston Park site during the second half of the 20th century may prevent temperatures below −10 °C (14 °F) occurring.

The coldest temperature to be recorded was −8.2 °C (17.2 °F) in 2010.[[90]](#cite_note-91) (Note: The official Weston Park Weather Station statistics, which can also be viewed at [Sheffield Central Library](/wiki/Sheffield_Central_Library "Sheffield Central Library"), has the temperature at −8.7 °C (16.3 °F), recorded on 20 December, and states that to be the lowest December temperature since 1981.) The coldest temperature ever recorded in the city of Sheffield at [Weston Park](/wiki/Weston_Park,_Sheffield "Weston Park, Sheffield"), since records began in 1882, is −14.6 °C (5.7 °F), registered in February 1895.[[91]](#cite_note-92) The lowest daytime maximum temperature in the city since records began is −5.6 °C (21.9 °F), also recorded in February 1895.[[92]](#cite_note-93) More recently, −4.4 °C (24.1 °F) was recorded as a daytime maximum at Weston Park, on 20 December 2010 (from the Weston Park Weather Station statistics, which also can be viewed at Sheffield Central Library.) On average, through the winter months of December to March, there are 67 days during which ground frost occurs.[[86]](#cite_note-Met_NE_England-87)

The Weston Park Weather station, established in 1882, is one of the longest running weather stations in the United Kingdom. It has recorded weather for more than 125 years, and a 2008 report showed that the climate of Sheffield is warming faster than it has at any time during this period, with 1990 and 2006 being the hottest years on record.[[98]](#cite_note-99) In collaboration with the [Stockholm Environment Institute](/wiki/Stockholm_Environment_Institute "Stockholm Environment Institute"), Sheffield developed a [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint") (based on 2004–05 consumption figures) of 5,798,361 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonnes "Tonnes") per year. This compares to the UK's total carbon footprint of 698,568,010 tonnes per year. The factors with the greatest impact are housing (34%), transport (25%), consumer (11%), private services (9%), public services (8%), food (8%) and capital investment (5%).[[99]](#cite_note-100) Sheffield City Council has signed up to the [10:10](/wiki/10:10 "10:10") campaign.[[100]](#cite_note-planet-101)

### Green belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Green belt")]

Further information: [South and West Yorkshire Green Belt](/wiki/South_and_West_Yorkshire_Green_Belt "South and West Yorkshire Green Belt") and [Geography of Sheffield](/wiki/Geography_of_Sheffield "Geography of Sheffield")

Sheffield is within a [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt_(United_Kingdom) "Green belt (United Kingdom)") region that extends into the wider surrounding counties, and is in place to reduce [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), prevent the towns and areas in the [Sheffield built-up area](/wiki/Sheffield_urban_area "Sheffield urban area") conurbation from further convergence, protect the identity of outlying communities, encourage [brownfield](/wiki/Brownfield "Brownfield") reuse, and preserve nearby countryside. This is achieved by restricting inappropriate development within the designated areas, and imposing stricter conditions on permitted building.[[101]](#cite_note-belt1-102)[[102]](#cite_note-belt2-103) The main urban area and larger villages of the borough are exempt from the green belt area, but surrounding smaller villages, hamlets and rural areas are 'washed over' with the designation. A subsidiary aim of the green belt is to encourage recreation and leisure interests,[[101]](#cite_note-belt1-102) with many rural landscape features and facilities included.

### Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

Main article: [Areas of Sheffield](/wiki/Areas_of_Sheffield "Areas of Sheffield")

Sheffield is made up of many suburbs and neighbourhoods, many of which developed from villages or [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)") that were absorbed into Sheffield as the city grew.[[17]](#cite_note-VICKERS-18) These historical areas are largely ignored by the modern administrative and political divisions of the city; instead it is divided into 28 electoral [wards](/wiki/Ward_(politics) "Ward (politics)"), with each ward generally covering 4–6 areas.[[103]](#cite_note-104) These electoral wards are grouped into six [parliamentary constituencies](/wiki/United_Kingdom_constituencies "United Kingdom constituencies"). Sheffield is largely [unparished](/wiki/Civil_parishes_in_England "Civil parishes in England"), but [Bradfield](/wiki/Bradfield,_South_Yorkshire "Bradfield, South Yorkshire") and [Ecclesfield](/wiki/Ecclesfield "Ecclesfield") have parish councils, and [Stocksbridge](/wiki/Stocksbridge "Stocksbridge") has a town council.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

Population of Sheffield from 1700 to 2011. The exponential population growth during the 19th century and the subsequent plateauing during the 20th century are evident.

The [2001 United Kingdom Census](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_Census "2001 United Kingdom Census") reported a resident population for Sheffield of 513,234, a 2% decline from the 1991 census.[[106]](#cite_note-Sheffield_2001-107) The city is part of the wider [Sheffield urban area](/wiki/Sheffield_urban_area "Sheffield urban area"), which had a population of 640,720.[[107]](#cite_note-SUA_2001-108) [In 2021](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census") the racial composition of Sheffield's population was 79.1% [White](/wiki/White_people "White people") (74.5% [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), 0.5% [White Irish](/wiki/Irish_migration_to_Great_Britain "Irish migration to Great Britain"), 0.5% [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people_in_the_United_Kingdom "Romani people in the United Kingdom") or [Irish Traveller](/wiki/Irish_Traveller "Irish Traveller"), 3.6% [Other White](/wiki/Other_White "Other White")), 3.5% of [mixed race](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)") (1.4% White and Black Caribbean, 0.4% White and Black African, 0.9% White and Asian, 0.8% Other Mixed), 9.6% [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") (1.2% [Indian](/wiki/British_Indian "British Indian"), 5% [Pakistani](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis"), 0.8% [Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi"), 1.3% [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese"), 1.3% Other Asian), 4.6% [Black](/wiki/Black_British "Black British") (3.3% African, 0.8% [Caribbean](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people"), 0.5% [Other Black](/wiki/Other_Black "Other Black")), 1.6% [Arab](/wiki/British_Arab "British Arab") and 1.5% of other ethnic heritage.[[108]](#cite_note-109) In terms of religion, 38% of the population are Christian, 10% are Muslim, 0.7% are Hindu, 0.4% are Buddhist, 0.2% are Sikh, 0.1% are Jewish, 0.5% belong to another religion, 43% have no religion and 6% did not state their religion.[[109]](#cite_note-110) The largest [quinary](/wiki/Quinary "Quinary") group is 20- to 24-year-olds (9%) because of the large university student population.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

The [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") served as a catalyst for considerable population growth and demographic change in Sheffield. Large numbers of people were driven to the city as the [cutlery](/wiki/Cutlery "Cutlery") and steel industries flourished. The population continued to grow until the mid-20th century, at which point, due to industrial decline, the population began to contract. However, by the early 21st century, the population had begun to grow once again.

The population of Sheffield previously peaked in 1951 at 577,050, and was declining steadily until the start of the century. The mid-2007 population estimate was 530,300, representing an increase of about 17,000 residents since 2001. The population of Sheffield has been increasing since.[[111]](#cite_note-112)

Although a city, Sheffield is informally known as "the [largest village in England](/wiki/Largest_village_in_England "Largest village in England")",[[112]](#cite_note-BaldwinBottomsWalker-113)[[113]](#cite_note-Binfield-114)[[114]](#cite_note-BurgoyneClark-115) because of a combination of topographical isolation and demographic stability.[[112]](#cite_note-BaldwinBottomsWalker-113) It is relatively geographically isolated, being cut off from other places by a ring of hills.[[115]](#cite_note-Hampton-116)[[116]](#cite_note-TaylorEvansFraser-117) Local folklore insists that, like Rome, Sheffield was built "on seven hills".[[116]](#cite_note-TaylorEvansFraser-117) The land surrounding Sheffield was unsuitable for industrial use,[[112]](#cite_note-BaldwinBottomsWalker-113) and now includes several protected [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt "Green belt") areas.[[117]](#cite_note-Mann-118) These topographical factors have served to restrict urban spread,[[117]](#cite_note-Mann-118) resulting in a relatively stable population size and a low degree of mobility.
