Main article: [Economy of Sheffield](/wiki/Economy_of_Sheffield "Economy of Sheffield")

See also: [List of companies in Sheffield](/wiki/List_of_companies_in_Sheffield "List of companies in Sheffield")

[![St Pauls is a new, mixed use development which forms part of the St Paul's Place development. In the top left corner is the Main St Paul's tower. Below it is the Tower 2, connected to the main tower but half the height. All have been completed within the last 5 years and represent some of the newest architecture in the city.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/St_Paul%27s_Place_%28geograph_7050524%29.jpg/250px-St_Paul%27s_Place_%28geograph_7050524%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Paul%27s_Place_(geograph_7050524).jpg)

[St Paul's Place](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Place "St Paul's Place"), 2010. [St Paul's Tower](/wiki/St_Pauls_Tower "St Pauls Tower"), the tallest building in Sheffield, is in the centre. The St Paul's Place development constitutes a major redevelopment of the area and has attracted numerous large companies to the complex, such as [DLA Piper](/wiki/DLA_Piper "DLA Piper"), [PwC](/wiki/PwC "PwC") and [Barclays](/wiki/Barclays "Barclays"). The [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education") and the [Department for Business, Innovation & Skills](/wiki/Department_for_Business,_Innovation_%26_Skills "Department for Business, Innovation & Skills") have also established a presence within St Paul's Place.

After many years of decline, the Sheffield economy is going through a strong revival. The 2004 [Barclays Bank](/wiki/Barclays_Bank "Barclays Bank") Financial Planning study[[118]](#cite_note-119) revealed that, in 2003, the Sheffield district of Hallam was the highest ranking area outside London for overall wealth, the proportion of people earning over £60,000 a year standing at almost 12%. A survey by [Knight Frank](/wiki/Knight_Frank "Knight Frank")[[119]](#cite_note-120) revealed that Sheffield was the fastest-growing city outside London for office and residential space and rents during the second half of 2004. This can be seen in a surge of redevelopments, including the [City Lofts Tower](/wiki/St_Pauls_Tower "St Pauls Tower") and accompanying [St Paul's Place](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Place "St Paul's Place"), Velocity Living and the Moor redevelopment,[[120]](#cite_note-121) the forthcoming [NRQ](/wiki/New_Retail_Quarter "New Retail Quarter") and the [Winter Gardens](/wiki/Sheffield_Winter_Gardens "Sheffield Winter Gardens"), [Peace Gardens](/wiki/Peace_Gardens "Peace Gardens"), [Millennium Galleries](/wiki/Millennium_Galleries "Millennium Galleries") and many projects completed under the [Sheffield One](/wiki/Sheffield_One "Sheffield One") redevelopment agency. The Sheffield economy grew from £5.6 billion in 1997 (1997 [GVA](/wiki/Gross_value_added "Gross value added"))[[121]](#cite_note-122) to £9.2 billion in 2007 (2007 GVA).[[122]](#cite_note-123)

The "UK Cities Monitor 2008" placed Sheffield among the top ten "best cities to locate a business today", the city occupying third and fourth places, respectively, for best office location and best new [call centre](/wiki/Call_centre "Call centre") location. The same report places Sheffield in third place regarding "greenest reputation" and second in terms of the availability of financial incentives.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

### Heavy industries and metallurgy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Heavy industries and metallurgy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Harry_Brearley.jpg/250px-Harry_Brearley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harry_Brearley.jpg)

Monument to [Harry Brearley](/wiki/Harry_Brearley "Harry Brearley") and the birthplace of [stainless steel](/wiki/Stainless_steel "Stainless steel") at the former [Brown Firth Research Laboratories](/wiki/Firth_Brown_Steels "Firth Brown Steels")

Sheffield has an international reputation for metallurgy and steel-making.[[124]](#cite_note-125) The earliest official record of cutlery production, for which Sheffield is particularly well known, is from 1297 when a tax return for 'Robert the Cutler' was submitted.[[125]](#cite_note-welcometosheffield.co.uk-126) A key reason for Sheffield's success in the production of cutlery lies in its geographic makeup. The abundance of streams in the area provided water power and the geological formations in the [Hope Valley](/wiki/Hope_Valley,_Derbyshire "Hope Valley, Derbyshire"), in particular, provided sufficient grit stones for grinding wheels.[[125]](#cite_note-welcometosheffield.co.uk-126) In the 17th century, the [Company of Cutlers in Hallamshire](/wiki/Company_of_Cutlers_in_Hallamshire "Company of Cutlers in Hallamshire"), which oversaw the booming cutlery industry in the area and remains to this day, was established and focused on markets outside the Sheffield area, leading to the gradual establishment of Sheffield as a respected producer of cutlery.[[125]](#cite_note-welcometosheffield.co.uk-126) this gradually developed from a national reputation into an international one.[[125]](#cite_note-welcometosheffield.co.uk-126)

Playing a crucial role in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), the city became an industrial powerhouse in the 18th century, and was dubbed "[Steel City](/wiki/Steel_City "Steel City")".[[126]](#cite_note-Steel_BBC-127) Many innovations in these fields have been made in Sheffield, for example [Benjamin Huntsman](/wiki/Benjamin_Huntsman "Benjamin Huntsman") discovered the [crucible technique](/wiki/Crucible_technique "Crucible technique") in the 1740s at his workshop in [Handsworth](/wiki/Handsworth,_South_Yorkshire "Handsworth, South Yorkshire").[[127]](#cite_note-128) This process was rendered obsolete in 1856 by [Henry Bessemer](/wiki/Henry_Bessemer "Henry Bessemer")'s invention of the [Bessemer converter](/wiki/Bessemer_converter "Bessemer converter"). [Thomas Boulsover](/wiki/Thomas_Boulsover "Thomas Boulsover") invented [Sheffield plate](/wiki/Sheffield_plate "Sheffield plate") (silver-plated copper) in the early 18th century.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

[Stainless steel](/wiki/Stainless_steel "Stainless steel") was invented by [Harry Brearley](/wiki/Harry_Brearley "Harry Brearley") in 1912, bringing affordable [cutlery](/wiki/Cutlery "Cutlery") to the masses.[[126]](#cite_note-Steel_BBC-127)[[129]](#cite_note-130) The work of [F. B. Pickering](/wiki/Frederick_Brian_Pickering "Frederick Brian Pickering") and T. Gladman throughout the 1960s, 1970s and 1980s was fundamental to the development of modern high-strength low-alloy steels.[[130]](#cite_note-131) Further innovations continue, with new advanced manufacturing technologies and techniques being developed on the [Advanced Manufacturing Park](/wiki/Advanced_Manufacturing_Park "Advanced Manufacturing Park"), situated just over the boundary in the borough of Rotherham, by Sheffield's universities and other independent research organisations.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Organisations located on the AMP include the Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre[[132]](#cite_note-AMCYP-133) (AMRC, a research partnership between the [Boeing Company](/wiki/Boeing_Company "Boeing Company")[[133]](#cite_note-134) and the [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield")), Castings Technology International (CTI), [The Welding Institute](/wiki/The_Welding_Institute "The Welding Institute") (TWI),[[134]](#cite_note-135) [Rolls-Royce plc](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_plc "Rolls-Royce plc") and [McLaren Automotive](/wiki/McLaren_Automotive "McLaren Automotive").[[132]](#cite_note-AMCYP-133)

[![Photo of the Forgemasters steel works, Sheffield](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Sheffield_Forgemasters_-_geograph.org.uk_-_517979.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Forgemasters_-_geograph.org.uk_-_517979.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Forgemasters_-_geograph.org.uk_-_517979.jpg)

[Forgemasters](/wiki/Forgemasters "Forgemasters") steel works in Sheffield. The site was formerly run by [Vickers Limited](/wiki/Vickers_Limited "Vickers Limited") which was founded in Sheffield in 1828 and became one of the most prominent engineering companies in the world.

[Forgemasters](/wiki/Sheffield_Forgemasters "Sheffield Forgemasters"), founded in 1805, was the sole remaining independent [steel works](/wiki/Steel_works "Steel works") left in the world and dominates the north-east of Sheffield around the [Lower Don Valley](/wiki/Lower_Don_Valley "Lower Don Valley").[[135]](#cite_note-136) The firm has a global reputation for producing the largest and most complex steel forgings and castings and is certified to produce critical nuclear components, with recent projects including the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy")'s [*Astute*-class](/wiki/Astute-class_submarine "Astute-class submarine") submarines.[[136]](#cite_note-137) The firm also has the capacity for pouring the largest single ingot (570 tonnes) in Europe and is currently in the process of expanding its capabilities.[[137]](#cite_note-138) In July 2021 Forgemasters was bought outright by the UK [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") for £2.56 million, with the intention of investing a further £400 million over the next decade.[[138]](#cite_note-theguardian1-139) The decision was based on the important role Forgemasters plays in the construction of the UK nuclear submarine fleet as well other vessels for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").[[138]](#cite_note-theguardian1-139)

While iron and steel have long been the main industries of Sheffield, [coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") has also been a major industry, particularly in the outlying areas,[[139]](#cite_note-140) and the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") in London was built using [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") from [quarries](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry") in the nearby village of [Anston](/wiki/Anston "Anston").[[140]](#cite_note-141)

### Public sector

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Public sector")]

[![University of Sheffield Diamond](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Diamond_UoS_2_26_May_2017.jpg/250px-Diamond_UoS_2_26_May_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diamond_UoS_2_26_May_2017.jpg)

*The Diamond*, at the University of Sheffield. The university is a major public sector employer in the city.

Sheffield has a large public sector workforce, numbering 77,500 workers. During the period 1995–2008 (a period of growth for the city and many others in the UK), the number of jobs in the city increased by 22% and 50% of these were in the public sector.[[141]](#cite_note-142) Major public sector employers include the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service"), The University of Sheffield, [Sheffield Hallam University](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_University "Sheffield Hallam University"), and numerous government departments and agencies including the [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office") (Visas & Immigration), [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education") & [Department for Business, Innovation & Skills](/wiki/Department_for_Business,_Innovation_%26_Skills "Department for Business, Innovation & Skills") have Offices in St Paul's Place and [Riverside Exchange](/wiki/Riverside_Exchange "Riverside Exchange").

[Sheffield City Council](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council "Sheffield City Council") employs over 8,000 people, spread across four different sections (known as portfolios). Sheffield City Council is also the [Local Education Authority](/wiki/Local_Education_Authority "Local Education Authority") (LEA) and as such manages all states schools and their associated staff. As part of its mandate to provide public services, Sheffield City Council maintains contracts with three private contractors—[Amey](/wiki/Amey_plc "Amey plc"), [Veolia](/wiki/Veolia "Veolia") and [Capita](/wiki/Capita "Capita").[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

### Leisure and retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Leisure and retail")]

#### City centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: City centre")]

[![An aerial view of Sheffield City Centre](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/SheffieldCitySkyline.jpg/250px-SheffieldCitySkyline.jpg)](/wiki/File:SheffieldCitySkyline.jpg)

A view of [Sheffield City Centre](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Centre "Sheffield City Centre"). Some of the major shopping precincts can be seen in the left and centre of the image.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/The_Moor%2C_Sheffield.jpg/250px-The_Moor%2C_Sheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Moor,_Sheffield.jpg)

The Moor

Sheffield is a major retail centre, and is home to many [high street](/wiki/High_street "High street") and [department stores](/wiki/Department_store "Department store") as well as designer boutiques.[[142]](#cite_note-143) The main shopping areas in the city centre are on [The Moor](/wiki/The_Moor_Quarter "The Moor Quarter") precinct, [Fargate](/wiki/Fargate "Fargate"), [Orchard Square](/wiki/Orchard_Square "Orchard Square") and the [Devonshire Quarter](/wiki/Devonshire_Quarter "Devonshire Quarter"). Department stores in the city centre include [Marks and Spencer](/wiki/Marks_and_Spencer "Marks and Spencer") and [Atkinsons](/wiki/Atkinsons "Atkinsons"). Sheffield's main market was once [Castle Market](/wiki/Castle_Market "Castle Market"), built above the remains of the castle. This has since been demolished.[[143]](#cite_note-144) [Sheffield Moor Market](/wiki/The_Moor_Quarter#Moor_Market "The Moor Quarter") opened in 2013 and became the main destination for fresh produce. The market has 196 stalls and includes local and organic produce, as well as international fusion cuisine such as Russian, Jamaican and Thai.[[144]](#cite_note-145) In March 2021 it was announced that the Sheffield branch of [John Lewis](/wiki/John_Lewis_%26_Partners "John Lewis & Partners") would close due to falling sales and a move to online shopping, which had increased because of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"). John Lewis received £3 million of public funding from Sheffield City Council in 2020 to keep the local store open.[[145]](#cite_note-146) The local [Debenhams](/wiki/Debenhams "Debenhams") branches are expected to re-open after the lifting of the 2021 COVID-19 lockdown restrictions, but only to clear existing stock, after which it is expected the stores will close.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148)

With the decline in high street shopping around the UK, efforts have been made to rejuvenate Sheffield City Centre and improve the retail and leisure offering. Major developments include [Leopold Square](/wiki/Leopold_Square "Leopold Square"), [The Moor](/wiki/The_Moor_Quarter "The Moor Quarter"), St Paul's Place (a mixed use development) and the [Heart of the City](/wiki/Heart_of_the_City,_Sheffield "Heart of the City, Sheffield") I & II projects. In March 2022 Sheffield City Council announced that a new leisure hub would be constructed at the southern end of Fargate. The £300,000 hub will feature cafes, shops and large-screen TVs for sports events.[[148]](#cite_note-149) The development is also related to other efforts to rejuvenate the Fargate area, such as a new mixed-use events and coworking hub at 20–26 Fargate, also overseen by Sheffield City Council.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

#### Shopping centres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Shopping centres")]

[![Meadowhall (shopping centre)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Meadowhall%2C_Sheffield_%28geograph_1994609%29.jpg/250px-Meadowhall%2C_Sheffield_%28geograph_1994609%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meadowhall,_Sheffield_(geograph_1994609).jpg)

[Meadowhall](/wiki/Meadowhall_(shopping_centre) "Meadowhall (shopping centre)")

[Meadowhall](/wiki/Meadowhall_(shopping_centre) "Meadowhall (shopping centre)") shopping centre, located to the north-east of Sheffield close to the boundary with [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham") and next to the [M1 motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway"), is a major regional shopping destination and currently ranked [eleventh largest](/wiki/List_of_shopping_centres_in_the_United_Kingdom_by_size "List of shopping centres in the United Kingdom by size") in the UK with a floorspace of 1.5 million ft2 (140,000 m2). Attracting over 30 million visitors a year (up from 19 million in its first year), the centre hosts 270 shops, 37 restaurants and a cinema.[[150]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-151)[[151]](#cite_note-152) Many nationally renowned brands have a presence at the centre including [Marks & Spencer](/wiki/Marks_%26_Spencer "Marks & Spencer"), [Hugo Boss](/wiki/Hugo_Boss "Hugo Boss") and [Jaeger](/wiki/Jaeger_(clothing) "Jaeger (clothing)"). The centre is connected to the city centre by rail, [Supertram](/wiki/Supertram_(Sheffield) "Supertram (Sheffield)") and bus services.[[150]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-151) Prior to the opening of Meadowhall, the site was occupied for East Hecla (steel) works, a major employer in the north-east of the city. The opening of Meadowhall in 1990 marked the beginning of major rejuvenation in the [Lower Don Valley](/wiki/Lower_Don_Valley "Lower Don Valley") as the steel industry contracted. In a 2010 survey of forecast expenditure at retail centres in the United Kingdom, Meadowhall was ranked 12th and [Sheffield City Centre](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Centre "Sheffield City Centre") 19th.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Ikea%2C_Sheffield.jpg/250px-Ikea%2C_Sheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ikea,_Sheffield.jpg)

IKEA, Sheffield

To the South of Meadowhall shopping centre is Meadowhall Retail Park, a 190,500-square-foot (17,700 m2) retail park with 13 retail and food units.[[153]](#cite_note-154) Next to the retail park is the Sheffield [IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA") store, opened in 2017. The opening ceremony was attended by dignitaries including the Swedish Ambassador to the UK.[[154]](#cite_note-155) The Sheffield store was the 20th opened in the UK and led to the creation of 480 new local jobs.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

The second-largest shopping centre in Sheffield is [Crystal Peaks](/wiki/Crystal_Peaks "Crystal Peaks"), located in the south-east of the city, alongside Drakehouse Retail Park. Both the shopping centre and the retail park opened in 1988 and now attract around 11 million visitors a year.[[156]](#cite_note-157) In total there are 101 retailers (including eateries) at Crystal Peaks and Drakehouse, including a range of high street brands. Crystal Peaks also includes a travel interchange which serves as the hub for bus travel in the east and south-east of Sheffield.

#### Suburbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Suburbs")]

[![Ecclesall Road, Sheffield](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Ecclesall_Road%2C_Sheffield_%281%29.jpg/250px-Ecclesall_Road%2C_Sheffield_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ecclesall_Road,_Sheffield_(1).jpg)

Ecclesall Road

[![Little Kelham in the Kelham Island Quarter](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Little_Kelham_buildings%2C_Sheffield%2C_UK.jpg/250px-Little_Kelham_buildings%2C_Sheffield%2C_UK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Little_Kelham_buildings,_Sheffield,_UK.jpg)

Little Kelham in the Kelham Island Quarter

Beyond the city centre there are numerous other leisure and shopping areas. To the south-west of the city centre is [Ecclesall Road](/wiki/Ecclesall_Road "Ecclesall Road"), a major thoroughfare connecting the south-western suburbs to the city centre and lined with bars, restaurants and cafes, as well as housing.[[157]](#cite_note-158) The area has a large student community owing to the presence of the Sheffield Hallam University Collegiate Campus adjacent to Ecclesall Road. The leisure section of the road is approximately 1.6 mi (2.5 km) long, with the south-western end becoming Ecclesall Road South and a predominantly
residential area. Another popular shopping and leisure area is [London Road](/wiki/London_Road_(Sheffield) "London Road (Sheffield)"), to the south of the city centre. The road is famous for its multicultural community which has led to an abundance of international cuisines being served at restaurants along the road. To the west of the city centre is [Broomhill](/wiki/Broomhill_and_Sharrow_Vale "Broomhill and Sharrow Vale"), a student-centric neighbourhood which also caters for school students as well local university students and NHS staff. To the north-west of the city centre are [Hillsborough](/wiki/Hillsborough,_Sheffield "Hillsborough, Sheffield"), a large retail and sports hub, and [Stocksbridge](/wiki/Stocksbridge "Stocksbridge") Fox Valley, a modern leisure and retail centre built on a brownfield industrial site.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

In the late 2010s and early 2020s several new developments began to the north of the city centre in the [Kelham Island Quarter](/wiki/Kelham_Island_Quarter "Kelham Island Quarter"), an increasingly popular mixed-use development. The area has become known for its independent cafes, restaurants and pubs and has seen significant residential development in recent years.

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Tourism plays a major role in the city's economy on account of [numerous attractions](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Sheffield "List of tourist attractions in Sheffield")—namely the Peak District, sports events (in particular, the [Snooker World Championships](/wiki/Snooker_World_Championships "Snooker World Championships")) and musical festivals (such as [Tramlines](/wiki/Tramlines "Tramlines")). In 2019, the tourism industry in Sheffield was valued at £1.36 billion and supported 15,000 jobs.[[159]](#cite_note-160)

In 2012, [Sheffield City Region Enterprise Zone](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Region_Enterprise_Zone "Sheffield City Region Enterprise Zone") was launched to promote development in a number of sites in Sheffield and across the wider region. In March 2014 additional sites were added to the zone.[[160]](#cite_note-161)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Sheffield](/wiki/Transport_in_Sheffield "Transport in Sheffield")

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Sheffield_Station_2022.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Station_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Station_2022.jpg)

Sheffield station

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Meadowhall_-_Interchange_and_Don_Footbridge.jpg/250px-Meadowhall_-_Interchange_and_Don_Footbridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meadowhall_-_Interchange_and_Don_Footbridge.jpg)

Meadowhall Interchange, a combined bus, coach, rail and tram interchange

[Sheffield station](/wiki/Sheffield_station "Sheffield station") is the principal interchange that serves the city. Services are operated by four [train operating companies](/wiki/Train_operating_companies "Train operating companies"): [East Midlands Railway](/wiki/East_Midlands_Railway "East Midlands Railway"), [CrossCountry](/wiki/CrossCountry "CrossCountry"), [TransPennine Express](/wiki/TransPennine_Express "TransPennine Express") and [Northern Trains](/wiki/Northern_Trains "Northern Trains").[[161]](#cite_note-162)

Major railway routes through Sheffield station include the [Midland Main Line](/wiki/Midland_Main_Line "Midland Main Line") to London via the East Midlands; the [Cross Country Route](/wiki/Cross_Country_Route "Cross Country Route"), which runs between eastern Scotland and south-west England; and the lines linking Liverpool and Manchester with [Hull](/wiki/Hull_railway_station "Hull railway station") and [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia").[[162]](#cite_note-163) With the redevelopment of [London St Pancras](/wiki/St_Pancras_railway_station "St Pancras railway station") completed, Sheffield has a direct connection to [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), via St Pancras and the [Eurostar](/wiki/Eurostar "Eurostar") to France and Belgium.[[163]](#cite_note-164) East Midlands Railway also operates three premium trains: the *[Master Cutler](/wiki/Master_Cutler_(train) "Master Cutler (train)")*, the *[Sheffield Continental](/wiki/Sheffield_Continental "Sheffield Continental")* and the *[South Yorkshireman](/wiki/South_Yorkshireman "South Yorkshireman")*.[[164]](#cite_note-165)

There are several local rail routes running along the city's valleys and beyond, connecting it with other parts of South Yorkshire, [West Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Yorkshire "West Yorkshire"), [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire"), [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") and [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire"). These local routes include the [Penistone Line](/wiki/Penistone_Line "Penistone Line"), the [Dearne Valley Line](/wiki/Dearne_Valley_Line "Dearne Valley Line"), the [Hope Valley Line](/wiki/Hope_Valley_Line "Hope Valley Line") and the [Hallam Line](/wiki/Hallam_Line "Hallam Line"). As well as the main stations of [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield_railway_station "Sheffield railway station") and Meadowhall, there are five suburban stations at [Chapeltown](/wiki/Chapeltown_railway_station "Chapeltown railway station"), [Darnall](/wiki/Darnall_railway_station "Darnall railway station"), [Woodhouse](/wiki/Woodhouse_railway_station "Woodhouse railway station") and [Dore & Totley](/wiki/Dore_%26_Totley_railway_station "Dore & Totley railway station").[[165]](#cite_note-166) As part of improvements to rail services along the Hope Valley Line between Sheffield and Manchester, a new platform, station facilities and track are being built at Dore & Totley; the expanded station opened in 2023.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

[High Speed 2](/wiki/High_Speed_2 "High Speed 2") had been planned to serve a city centre station in Sheffield as a spur from the main eastern HS2 line.[[167]](#cite_note-168) It was scheduled to be operational by 2033, with four trains an hour, reducing journey times to London and Birmingham to 1 hour 19 minutes and 48 minutes, respectively.[[168]](#cite_note-169) In November 2021, the UK government published the *Integrated Rail Plan for the North and Midlands* which announced HS2's eastern spur route (between the East Midlands and Leeds, including Sheffield) had been cancelled. The document announced upgrades to the Midland Mainline, with HS2 trains able to run on this upgraded and electrified route.[[169]](#cite_note-170)

### Trams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Trams")]

[![A Sheffield Supertram in current blue, orange and red Stagecoach livery.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Sheffield_Supertram_-_Tram_Train_399_204_%28geograph_5940823%29.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Supertram_-_Tram_Train_399_204_%28geograph_5940823%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Supertram_-_Tram_Train_399_204_(geograph_5940823).jpg)

A Sheffield Supertram

The [Sheffield Supertram](/wiki/Sheffield_Supertram "Sheffield Supertram") opened in 1994 and was operated by [Stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group"). The opening was shortly after the similar [Metrolink](/wiki/Manchester_Metrolink "Manchester Metrolink") scheme in Greater Manchester. The Supertram network consists of 37 mi (60 km) of track and four lines (with all lines running via the city centre): from Halfway to [Malin Bridge](/wiki/Malin_Bridge "Malin Bridge") (Blue Line), from Meadowhall to [Middlewood](/wiki/Middlewood,_South_Yorkshire "Middlewood, South Yorkshire") (Yellow Line), from Cathedral to [Herdings Park](/wiki/Herdings_Park "Herdings Park") (Purple Line),[[170]](#cite_note-171) and the from [Cathedral](/wiki/Sheffield_Cathedral "Sheffield Cathedral") to [Rotherham Parkgate](/wiki/Rotherham_Parkgate_tram_stop "Rotherham Parkgate tram stop") (Black Line). The system contains both on-street and segregated running, depending upon the section and line. The Black Line opened in 2018,[[171]](#cite_note-172) with *tram-trains*; these are trams that are able to share a line with conventional heavy rail trains between Sheffield and Rotherham.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

In March 2024, control of the network passed to the [South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Mayoral_Combined_Authority "South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority").[[173]](#cite_note-174)

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Roads")]

[![Aerial view of Park Square, where the Sheffield Parkway meets the Sheffield Inner Ring Road](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/View_of_Park_Square_from_Park_Hill.jpg/250px-View_of_Park_Square_from_Park_Hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Park_Square_from_Park_Hill.jpg)

Aerial view of Park Square, where the Sheffield Parkway meets the Sheffield Inner Ring Road

Motorways near the city are the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") and [M18](/wiki/M18_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M18 motorway (Great Britain)").[[174]](#cite_note-OS-175) [Sheffield Parkway](/wiki/Sheffield_Parkway "Sheffield Parkway") connects the city centre to the motorways. The M1 skirts the city's north-east and crossing [Tinsley Viaduct](/wiki/Tinsley_Viaduct "Tinsley Viaduct") near Rotherham. The M18 branches from the M1 close to Sheffield, linking the city with [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster") and ending at [Goole](/wiki/Goole "Goole").[[175]](#cite_note-176)

The [A57](/wiki/A57_road "A57 road") and [A61 roads](/wiki/A61_road "A61 road") are the major [trunk roads](/wiki/Trunk_road "Trunk road") through Sheffield.[[174]](#cite_note-OS-175) These run east–west and north–south, respectively, crossing in the city centre, from where the other major roads generally radiate spoke-like. An inner [ring road](/wiki/Sheffield_Inner_Ring_Road "Sheffield Inner Ring Road"), mostly constructed in the 1970s and extended in 2007 to form a complete ring,[[176]](#cite_note-177) allows traffic to avoid the city centre, and an [outer ring road](/wiki/Sheffield_Outer_Ring_Road "Sheffield Outer Ring Road") runs to the east, south-east and north, nearer the edge of the city, but does not serve the western side of Sheffield.[[174]](#cite_note-OS-175)

### Buses and coaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Buses and coaches")]

[![Sheffield bus and coach Interchange is in the foreground, in the bottom left corner is the main entrance. In the centre are long bus stands that form the interchange. Above that is the Digital Campus built upon an unused part of the interchange. In the top left corner is the 1960s Park Hill flats and to the right is Sheffield station.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Shefield_Interchange_2022.jpg/250px-Shefield_Interchange_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shefield_Interchange_2022.jpg)

Sheffield Interchange, served by National Express Coaches

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Wright_StreetDeck_Hybrid.jpg/250px-Wright_StreetDeck_Hybrid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wright_StreetDeck_Hybrid.jpg)

A First South Yorkshire service

[Sheffield Interchange](/wiki/Sheffield_Interchange "Sheffield Interchange") is the city's bus main hub; other bus stations are at [Halfway](/wiki/Halfway,_Sheffield "Halfway, Sheffield"), [Hillsborough](/wiki/Hillsborough,_South_Yorkshire "Hillsborough, South Yorkshire") and [Meadowhall](/wiki/Darnall#Meadowhall "Darnall"). Current providers are [First South Yorkshire](/wiki/First_South_Yorkshire "First South Yorkshire") (the largest operator),[Stagecoach Yorkshire](/wiki/Stagecoach_Yorkshire "Stagecoach Yorkshire"), [TM Travel](/wiki/TM_Travel "TM Travel"), [Hulleys of Baslow](/wiki/Hulleys_of_Baslow "Hulleys of Baslow") and Sheffield Community Transport.[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179)

There is also the [Bus Rapid Transit North](/wiki/Bus_Rapid_Transit_North "Bus Rapid Transit North") route between Sheffield and [Maltby](/wiki/Maltby,_South_Yorkshire "Maltby, South Yorkshire") via Rotherham. It was planned as two routes: the Northern route to Rotherham via Meadowhall and [Templeborough](/wiki/Templeborough "Templeborough"), and the southern route via the developing employment centre and Waverley.[[179]](#cite_note-180) The northern route opened in September 2016; it involved an 800-metre Tinsley Road Link to be built between Meadowhall and the A6178 road.[[180]](#cite_note-181) [Yorkshire Terrier](/wiki/Yorkshire_Terrier_(bus_company) "Yorkshire Terrier (bus company)"), Andrews and the parent company [Yorkshire Traction](/wiki/Yorkshire_Traction "Yorkshire Traction") formerly operated in the city and were taken-over by [Stagecoach Sheffield](/wiki/Stagecoach_Sheffield "Stagecoach Sheffield").[[181]](#cite_note-182) [Stagecoach Group](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group") also operates the [Supertram](/wiki/Sheffield_Supertram "Sheffield Supertram") and has an integrated ticketing system with buses and tram.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

[Coach](/wiki/Coach_(vehicle) "Coach (vehicle)") services running through Sheffield are operated by [National Express](/wiki/National_Express_Coaches "National Express Coaches") and to a lesser extent [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(Europe) "Megabus (Europe)") and [Flixbus](/wiki/Flixbus "Flixbus"). National Express services call at Sheffield Interchange, Meadowhall Interchange and Meadowhead Bus Stop. Megabus and Flixbus services only call at Meadowhall. National Express services 564, 560, 350, 320, 310 and 240 call at Sheffield, as do others on a less frequent basis.[[183]](#cite_note-184) The 560/564 service is a direct connection to London [Victoria Coach Station](/wiki/Victoria_Coach_Station "Victoria Coach Station") via Chesterfield and [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes_Coachway "Milton Keynes Coachway"), operating 12 times a day in both directions. The 350 and 240 services connect Sheffield to [Manchester Airport](/wiki/Manchester_Airport "Manchester Airport") and [Heathrow](/wiki/Heathrow "Heathrow")/[Gatwick](/wiki/Gatwick "Gatwick") Airports, respectively.[[184]](#cite_note-185) Two Megabus services, the M12 and M20, call at Sheffield en route to London from [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") and [Inverness](/wiki/Inverness "Inverness"), respectively.[[185]](#cite_note-186)

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Cycling")]

Main article: [cycling in South Yorkshire](/wiki/Cycling_in_South_Yorkshire "Cycling in South Yorkshire")

Although hilly, Sheffield is compact and [cycling in Sheffield](/wiki/Cycling_in_South_Yorkshire "Cycling in South Yorkshire") is growing as a method of transport. It is on the [Trans-Pennine Trail](/wiki/Trans-Pennine_Trail "Trans-Pennine Trail"), a [National Cycle Network](/wiki/National_Cycle_Network "National Cycle Network") route running from west to east from [Southport](/wiki/Southport "Southport") in Merseyside to [Hornsea](/wiki/Hornsea "Hornsea") in the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire") and north to south from [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") in West Yorkshire to [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield,_Derbyshire "Chesterfield, Derbyshire") in Derbyshire.[[186]](#cite_note-187) There are many cycle routes going along country paths in the woods surrounding the city, and a network of quality cycle tracks and low traffic streets is slowly being developed in the city centre.

### Canal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Canal")]

[![Victoria Quays, Sheffield Canal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Victoria_Quays_-_Sheffield.jpg/250px-Victoria_Quays_-_Sheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Quays_-_Sheffield.jpg)

Victoria Quays

The [Sheffield & South Yorkshire Navigation](/wiki/Sheffield_%26_South_Yorkshire_Navigation "Sheffield & South Yorkshire Navigation") (S&SY) is a system of navigable inland waterways ([canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") and canalised rivers) in Yorkshire and [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire").[[187]](#cite_note-188) Chiefly based on the [River Don](/wiki/River_Don,_South_Yorkshire "River Don, South Yorkshire"), it runs for a length of 43 mi (69 km) and has 29 [locks](/wiki/Canal_lock "Canal lock"). It connects Sheffield, [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham") and [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster") with the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent") at [Keadby](/wiki/Keadby "Keadby") and (via the [New Junction Canal](/wiki/New_Junction_Canal "New Junction Canal")) the [Aire & Calder Navigation](/wiki/Aire_%26_Calder_Navigation "Aire & Calder Navigation").[[188]](#cite_note-189) The terminus of the canal is at [Victoria Quays](/wiki/Victoria_Quays "Victoria Quays"),[[189]](#cite_note-190) a redevelopment mixed-used area adjacent to Park Square in Sheffield City Centre.

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Air")]

The closest airports are: [Leeds Bradford](/wiki/Leeds_Bradford_Airport "Leeds Bradford Airport"), [Humberside](/wiki/Humberside_Airport "Humberside Airport"), [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands_Airport "East Midlands Airport") (within an hour's drive of the city) and [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_Airport "Manchester Airport") (hourly direct service by TransPennine Express).

Due to the topographical nature of the city, Sheffield was not served by its own airport. In May 1990, [Sheffield Development Corporation](/wiki/Sheffield_Development_Corporation "Sheffield Development Corporation") entered into an agreement with A. F. Budge (Mining) Ltd for the construction and operation of the airport and the development of adjacent land. In 1997, [Sheffield City Airport](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Airport "Sheffield City Airport") was opened on land close to the M1 and the Sheffield Parkway. The airport was operated on STOLPORT model similar to [London City Airport](/wiki/London_City_Airport "London City Airport") and operated a limited range of short range business focused flights to destinations in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"). The airport fell into decline with the growth of low cost airlines in the late 1990s and the last scheduled flight took place in 2002. The airport closed and lost its [Civil Aviation Authority](/wiki/Civil_Aviation_Authority_(United_Kingdom) "Civil Aviation Authority (United Kingdom)") licence in 2008.[[190]](#cite_note-191)

[Doncaster Sheffield Airport](/wiki/Doncaster_Sheffield_Airport "Doncaster Sheffield Airport") (also known as Robin Hood Airport) then became the closest international airport to Sheffield, located 18 mi (29 km) from the city centre. It opened on 28 April 2005 on the former [RAF Finningley](/wiki/RAF_Finningley "RAF Finningley") site and was served mainly by charter and budget airlines, with about one million passengers a year.[[191]](#cite_note-192) Destinations had included the Canary Islands, Balearics, Greece, Turkey, Poland and the Baltic countries with [TUI Airways](/wiki/TUI_Airways "TUI Airways") and [Wizz Air](/wiki/Wizz_Air "Wizz Air") operating from the airport. A link road, called the Great Yorkshire Way, connects Doncaster Sheffield Airport to the M18 motorway, reducing the journey time from Sheffield city centre from 40 to 25 minutes.[[192]](#cite_note-193) The airport closed in 2022. In June 2023 South Yorkshire's mayor [Oliver Coppard](/wiki/Oliver_Coppard "Oliver Coppard") handed Doncaster council £3.1 million to help the council build a case for legal action in pursuit of a compulsory purchase order, which it believes will cost up to £6.25 million.[[193]](#cite_note-194)[[194]](#cite_note-195)[[195]](#cite_note-196)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Sheffield](/wiki/Education_in_Sheffield "Education in Sheffield")

Within the city of Sheffield there are two universities, 141 primary schools and 28 secondary schools.[[196]](#cite_note-197)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Kelham_Island_Museum%2C_Sheffield_UK.jpg/250px-Kelham_Island_Museum%2C_Sheffield_UK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kelham_Island_Museum,_Sheffield_UK.jpg)

[Kelham Island Museum](/wiki/Kelham_Island_Museum "Kelham Island Museum") (left). The museum is home to The [Bessemer Converter](/wiki/Bessemer_converter "Bessemer converter"), the most important technique for making [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") from the 1850s to the 1950s.

Sheffield's museums are managed by two distinct organisations. [Museums Sheffield](/wiki/Museums_Sheffield "Museums Sheffield") manages the [Weston Park Museum](/wiki/Weston_Park_Museum "Weston Park Museum") (a Grade II\* listed Building), [Millennium Galleries](/wiki/Millennium_Galleries "Millennium Galleries") and [Graves Art Gallery](/wiki/Graves_Art_Gallery "Graves Art Gallery").[[197]](#cite_note-198) These museums constitute the oldest extant museums in the city, with Graves Art Gallery and Weston Park Museum being gifted to the city by industrialist philanthropists in the 19th and 20th centuries. The Millennium Galleries, being established in the early 2000s, is one of the newest museums and constitutes part of the Heart of the City development, connecting directly to the Winter Garden and Millennium Square.[[198]](#cite_note-199) All three museums host a broad range of exhibits which reflect Sheffield's history and numerous other themes, including exhibitions on loan from other major galleries and museums.

[Sheffield Industrial Museums Trust](/wiki/Sheffield_Industrial_Museums_Trust "Sheffield Industrial Museums Trust") manages the museums dedicated to Sheffield's industrial heritage of which there are three.[[199]](#cite_note-200) [Kelham Island Museum](/wiki/Kelham_Island_Museum "Kelham Island Museum") (located just to the North of the city centre) opened in 1982 and is located on the site of a 19th-century iron foundry and showcases the city's history of steel manufacturing and includes a range of important historical artifacts,[[200]](#cite_note-201) including a preserved [Bessemer Converter](/wiki/Bessemer_converter "Bessemer converter") (which won an Engineering Heritage Award in 2004 from the [Institution of Mechanical Engineers](/wiki/Institution_of_Mechanical_Engineers "Institution of Mechanical Engineers")), munitions and mechanical components from WW2 aircraft (Including a crankshaft from a [Spitfire](/wiki/Spitfire "Spitfire") which, during the early stages of the war, could only be produced in Sheffield) and a fully functional 12,000 horsepower steam engine dating to the 19th century.[[201]](#cite_note-202) The museum is an Anchor Point for the ERIH, [The European Route of Industrial Heritage](/wiki/European_Route_of_Industrial_Heritage "European Route of Industrial Heritage"). [Abbeydale Industrial Hamlet](/wiki/Abbeydale_Industrial_Hamlet "Abbeydale Industrial Hamlet") (in the south of the city) is a Grade I listed building and a [Scheduled Ancient Monument](/wiki/Scheduled_Ancient_Monument "Scheduled Ancient Monument").[[202]](#cite_note-203) [Shepherd Wheel](/wiki/Shepherd_Wheel "Shepherd Wheel") (in the south-East of the city) is a former water-powered grinding [workshop](/wiki/Workshop "Workshop"), Grade II listed and a [Scheduled Ancient Monument](/wiki/Scheduled_Ancient_Monument "Scheduled Ancient Monument").[[203]](#cite_note-204) Also there are [Sheffield Archives](/wiki/Sheffield_Archives "Sheffield Archives").

In August 2022 the Yorkshire Natural History Museum opened on Holme Lane in Sheffield. Many of the exhibits come from the collection of James Hogg and feature a collection of Jurassic marine life, such as [ammonites](/wiki/Ammonoidea "Ammonoidea"), [belemnites](/wiki/Belemnitida "Belemnitida"), [plesiosaurs](/wiki/Plesiosaurus "Plesiosaurus") and [ichthyosaurs](/wiki/Ichthyosauria "Ichthyosauria"), many of which were collected from the Lias of the Yorkshire Coast. The museum has Europe's first publicly accessible fossil preparation and conservation laboratory with ultrasonic preparation facilities, an acid preparation laboratory, 3D scanning, CT scanning and 3D printing.[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206) On the opening day palaeontologist Dean Lomax examined one of the fossils on display and declared it to be the oldest example of a vertebrate embryo found in Britain and the oldest complete ichthyosaur embryo ever found in Britain.[[206]](#cite_note-207)

There are also a number of independent museums in the city including the [National Videogame Museum](/wiki/National_Videogame_Museum_(United_Kingdom) "National Videogame Museum (United Kingdom)") and the [National Emergency Services Museum](/wiki/National_Emergency_Services_Museum "National Emergency Services Museum"),[[207]](#cite_note-208) as well as the University of Sheffield's [Turner Museum of Glass](/wiki/Turner_Museum_of_Glass "Turner Museum of Glass").[[208]](#cite_note-209)

### Universities, colleges and UTCs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Universities, colleges and UTCs")]

[![Red brick university building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Sheffield_Uni.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Uni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Uni.jpg)

[Firth Court](/wiki/Firth_Court "Firth Court"), the administrative and ceremonial centre of the University of Sheffield

The city's universities are [the University of Sheffield](/wiki/The_University_of_Sheffield "The University of Sheffield") and Sheffield Hallam University. The two combined bring about 60,000 students to the city every year.[[209]](#cite_note-HESA_citation-210) The University of Sheffield is the city's oldest university. It was established in 1897 as University College Sheffield and gained university status in 1905. Its history traces back to Sheffield Medical School found in 1828, Firth College in 1879 and Sheffield Technical School in 1884. The university is one of the original [red brick universities](/wiki/Red_brick_universities "Red brick universities") and is a member of the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Sheffield_City_Centre_and_station.jpg/250px-Sheffield_City_Centre_and_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_City_Centre_and_station.jpg)

Sheffield Hallam University city campus seen from South Street Park

[Sheffield Hallam University](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_University "Sheffield Hallam University") (SHU) is a university on two sites in Sheffield. City Campus is located in the city centre, close to Sheffield railway station, and Collegiate Crescent Campus is about 2 mi (3.2 km) away, adjacent to [Ecclesall Road](/wiki/Ecclesall_Road "Ecclesall Road") in south-west Sheffield. Sheffield Hallam University's history goes back to 1843 with the establishment of the Sheffield School of Design. During the 1960s several independent colleges (including the School of Design) joined to become Sheffield Polytechnic (Sheffield City Polytechnic from 1976) and was finally renamed Sheffield Hallam University in 1992.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park.jpg)

[UTC Sheffield Olympic Legacy Park](/wiki/UTC_Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park "UTC Sheffield Olympic Legacy Park") (left) and Sheffield Hallam University (right)

Sheffield has three main [further education](/wiki/Further_education "Further education") providers: [The Sheffield College](/wiki/The_Sheffield_College "The Sheffield College"), [Longley Park Sixth Form](/wiki/Longley_Park_Sixth_Form "Longley Park Sixth Form") and [Chapeltown Academy](/wiki/Chapeltown_Academy "Chapeltown Academy"). [The Sheffield College](/wiki/The_Sheffield_College "The Sheffield College") is organised on a federal basis and was originally created from the merger of six colleges around the city: Sheffield City (formerly Castle),[[210]](#cite_note-211) Olive Grove and Eyre Street near the city centre, Hillsborough and Fir Vale, serving the north of the city and Peaks to the south.[[211]](#cite_note-212)

Launched by the [coalition government](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") in 2010, the [University Technical College](/wiki/University_Technical_College "University Technical College") programme was designed to foster greater interest in [STEM](/wiki/STEM "STEM") subjects amongst students aged 14 to 18. Sheffield currently hosts two UTCs, [UTC Sheffield City Centre](/wiki/UTC_Sheffield_City_Centre "UTC Sheffield City Centre") and [UTC Sheffield Olympic Legacy Park](/wiki/UTC_Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park "UTC Sheffield Olympic Legacy Park"). All UTCs, including those in Sheffield, are sponsored by the Baker Dearing Educational Trust,[[212]](#cite_note-bakertrust-213) established by [Lord Baker](/wiki/Kenneth_Baker,_Baron_Baker_of_Dorking "Kenneth Baker, Baron Baker of Dorking"). The two UTCs in Sheffield are also sponsored and supported by Sheffield Hallam University. Whilst the UTCs are equivalent to regular secondary schools and sixth forms, their governance structure and curriculum are different, owing to their status as free schools and focusing on STEM, as opposed to a broader curriculum.

### Secondary, primary and nursery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Secondary, primary and nursery")]

See also: [List of schools in Sheffield](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Sheffield "List of schools in Sheffield")

There are 137 primary schools, 26 secondary schools—of which 10 have [sixth forms](/wiki/Sixth_form "Sixth form"): ([High Storrs](/wiki/High_Storrs_School_(Sheffield) "High Storrs School (Sheffield)"), [King Ecgberts](/wiki/King_Ecgbert_School "King Ecgbert School"), [King Edward VII](/wiki/King_Edward_VII_School_(Sheffield) "King Edward VII School (Sheffield)"), [Silverdale](/wiki/Silverdale_School_(Sheffield) "Silverdale School (Sheffield)"), [Meadowhead](/wiki/Meadowhead_School "Meadowhead School"), [Tapton](/wiki/Tapton_School "Tapton School"), [Notre Dame Catholic High](/wiki/Notre_Dame_School_(Sheffield) "Notre Dame School (Sheffield)") and [All Saints Catholic High](/wiki/All_Saints_Catholic_High_School,_Sheffield "All Saints Catholic High School, Sheffield")[[213]](#cite_note-214))—and a sixth-form college, [Longley Park Sixth Form](/wiki/Longley_Park_Sixth_Form "Longley Park Sixth Form").[[214]](#cite_note-215) The city's five independent private schools include [Birkdale School](/wiki/Birkdale_School "Birkdale School") and the [Sheffield High School](/wiki/Sheffield_High_School_(South_Yorkshire) "Sheffield High School (South Yorkshire)").[[215]](#cite_note-216) There are also 12 special schools and a number of Integrated Resource Units in mainstream schools which are, along with all other schools, managed by Sheffield City Council.[[216]](#cite_note-sheffield2010-217) All schools are non-selective, mixed sex schools (apart from [Sheffield High School](/wiki/Sheffield_High_School,_South_Yorkshire "Sheffield High School, South Yorkshire") and Al-Mahad Al-Islami, both all-girls schools).[[216]](#cite_note-sheffield2010-217)[[217]](#cite_note-218) The Early Years Education and Childcare Service of Sheffield City Council manages 32 nurseries and children's centres in the city.[[216]](#cite_note-sheffield2010-217)

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Religion")]

Sheffield is home to a centre of multicultural events, institutions, and places of worship. Some of the city's most notable buildings include its main [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") [Diocese of Sheffield](/wiki/Diocese_of_Sheffield "Diocese of Sheffield")'s [cathedral](/wiki/Sheffield_Cathedral "Sheffield Cathedral") on Church Street and the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [Diocese of Hallam](/wiki/Diocese_of_Hallam "Diocese of Hallam")'s [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral_Church_of_St_Marie,_Sheffield "Cathedral Church of St Marie, Sheffield") on Norfolk Row.[[218]](#cite_note-219)

The city also has other churches including [St Vincent's Church](/wiki/St_Vincent%27s_Church,_Sheffield "St Vincent's Church, Sheffield"), [St Matthew's Church](/wiki/St_Matthew%27s_Church,_Sheffield "St Matthew's Church, Sheffield"), [St Paul's Church](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Church,_Wordsworth_Avenue "St Paul's Church, Wordsworth Avenue"), [St Paul's Church and Centre](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Church_and_Centre,_Norton_Lees,_Sheffield "St Paul's Church and Centre, Norton Lees, Sheffield"), [Victoria Hall](/wiki/Victoria_Hall,_Sheffield "Victoria Hall, Sheffield") and [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Fulwood,_Sheffield "Christ Church, Fulwood, Sheffield").[[219]](#cite_note-220) Other places of worship include the [Madina Mosque](/wiki/Madina_Mosque_(Sheffield) "Madina Mosque (Sheffield)"), [Sheffield & District Reform Jewish Congregation](/wiki/Sheffield_%26_District_Reform_Jewish_Congregation "Sheffield & District Reform Jewish Congregation") (Progressive) and [Kingfield Synagogue](/wiki/Kingfield_Synagogue "Kingfield Synagogue") (Orthodox).
