## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in Sheffield](/wiki/Sport_in_Sheffield "Sport in Sheffield")

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Bramall_Lane_End.jpg/250px-Bramall_Lane_End.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bramall_Lane_End.jpg)

[Bramall Lane](/wiki/Bramall_Lane "Bramall Lane"), the home of [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C."), is close to the city centre.

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Hillsborough_Stadium_interior.jpg/250px-Hillsborough_Stadium_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hillsborough_Stadium_interior.jpg)

[Hillsborough](/wiki/Hillsborough_Stadium "Hillsborough Stadium"), the home of [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."), is the city's largest stadium with a capacity of just under 40,000.

Sheffield has a long sporting heritage, and the city claims to be the birthplace of professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football").[[220]](#cite_note-221) In 1857 a collective of [cricketers](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") formed the world's first-ever official football club, [Sheffield F.C.](/wiki/Sheffield_F.C. "Sheffield F.C."),[[221]](#cite_note-222) and the world's second-ever, [Hallam F.C.](/wiki/Hallam_F.C. "Hallam F.C."), who also play at the [world's oldest football ground](/wiki/Sandygate_Road "Sandygate Road")[[222]](#cite_note-223) in the suburb of [Crosspool](/wiki/Crosspool "Crosspool"). Sheffield and Hallam are today Sheffield's two major non-league sides, although Sheffield now play just outside the city in nearby [Dronfield](/wiki/Dronfield "Dronfield"), Derbyshire. Sheffield and Hallam contest what has become known as the Sheffield derby. By 1860 there were 15 football clubs in Sheffield, with the first ever amateur league and cup competitions taking place in the city.[[223]](#cite_note-224)

Sheffield is best known for its two professional football teams, [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C."), nicknamed *The Blades*, and [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."), nicknamed [*The Owls*](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C.#Nickname "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."). United, who play at [Bramall Lane](/wiki/Bramall_Lane "Bramall Lane") south of the city centre and Wednesday, who play at [Hillsborough](/wiki/Hillsborough_Stadium "Hillsborough Stadium") in the north-west of the city, both compete in the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") for the [2025–26 season](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_EFL_Championship "2025–26 EFL Championship"). The two clubs contest the [Steel City Derby](/wiki/Steel_City_Derby "Steel City Derby"), which is considered by many to be one of the most fierce football rivalries in English Football.[[224]](#cite_note-225)

In the pre-war era, both Wednesday and United enjoyed large amounts of success and found themselves two of the country's top clubs; Sheffield Wednesday have been champions of the Football League four times—in [1902–03](/wiki/1902%E2%80%9303_Football_League "1902–03 Football League"), [1903–04](/wiki/1903-04_Football_League "1903-04 Football League"), [1928–29](/wiki/1928-29_Football_League "1928-29 Football League") and [1929–30](/wiki/1929-30_Football_League "1929-30 Football League"), whilst Sheffield United have won it once, in [1897–98](/wiki/1897-98_Football_League "1897-98 Football League"). During the 1970s and early 1980s the two sides fell from grace, with Wednesday finding themselves in the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") by the mid-70s and United as far as the [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division") in [1981](/wiki/1980-81_in_English_football "1980-81 in English football"). Wednesday once again became one of England's high-flying clubs following promotion back to the [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") in [1984](/wiki/1983-84_in_English_football "1983-84 in English football"), winning the [League Cup in 1991](/wiki/1991_Football_League_Cup_Final "1991 Football League Cup Final"), competing in the [UEFA Cup in 1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_UEFA_Cup "1992–93 UEFA Cup"), and reaching the final of both the [League Cup](/wiki/1993_Football_League_Cup_Final "1993 Football League Cup Final") and [FA Cup](/wiki/1993_FA_Cup_Final "1993 FA Cup Final") in the [same season](/wiki/1992-93_in_English_football "1992-93 in English football").

United and Wednesday were both founding members of the [FA Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") in 1992, but The Blades were relegated in [1994](/wiki/1993-94_in_English_football "1993-94 in English football"). The Owls remained until [2000](/wiki/1999-00_in_English_football "1999-00 in English football"). Both clubs had gone into decline in the 21st century, Wednesday twice relegated to [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") and United suffering the same fate in [2011](/wiki/2010-11_in_English_football "2010-11 in English football"), despite a brief spell in the Premier League in [2006–07](/wiki/2006-07_in_English_football "2006-07 in English football"). United was promoted to the Premier League in 2019 under manager, and Sheffield United Fan, [Chris Wilder](/wiki/Chris_Wilder "Chris Wilder"). Despite being written off by most football pundits, and declared favourites for relegation from the Premier League, United exceeded expectations and finished in the top half of the table in the 2019–20 season. In the 2020–21 season, United sat at the bottom of the Premier League table by the conclusion of the season and were relegated.

Sheffield was the site of the deadliest sports venue disaster in the United Kingdom, the [Hillsborough disaster](/wiki/Hillsborough_disaster "Hillsborough disaster") in 1989, when 97 [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") supporters were killed in a stampede and crush during an [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") semi-final at the venue.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

[Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C."), who play in the Championship, did play their home games in the city between 2008 and 2012, having moved to play at Sheffield's [Don Valley Stadium](/wiki/Don_Valley_Stadium "Don Valley Stadium") in 2008 following a dispute with their previous landlord at their traditional home ground of [Millmoor](/wiki/Millmoor "Millmoor"), Rotherham.[[226]](#cite_note-227) However, in July 2012, the club moved to the new 12,000 seat [New York Stadium](/wiki/New_York_Stadium "New York Stadium") in Rotherham.[[227]](#cite_note-228) There are also facilities for golf, [climbing](/wiki/Climbing "Climbing") and bowling, as well as a newly inaugurated national ice-skating arena ([IceSheffield](/wiki/IceSheffield "IceSheffield")).

### Rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Rugby")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park_%28overview%29.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park_%28overview%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Park_(overview).jpg)

[Sheffield Olympic Legacy Park](/wiki/Olympic_Legacy_Park "Olympic Legacy Park"), home of the [Sheffield Eagles](/wiki/Sheffield_Eagles "Sheffield Eagles")

[Sheffield Eagles](/wiki/Sheffield_Eagles "Sheffield Eagles") RLFC are the city's professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") team and play their matches at [Sheffield Olympic Legacy Stadium](/wiki/Sheffield_Olympic_Legacy_Stadium "Sheffield Olympic Legacy Stadium"). They currently play in the second tier of the professional league, the [Championship](/wiki/Co-operative_Championship "Co-operative Championship") and won back to back titles in 2012 and 2013. Their most successful moment came in 1998, when, against all the odds they defeated [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") in the Challenge Cup final, despite being huge underdogs. The team then hit troubled times before reforming in 2003. Since then they have played their rugby in the Championship (second tier). In 2011, they made the playoffs finishing in fifth place. They made the Grand Final, by defeating [Leigh](/wiki/Leigh_Centurions "Leigh Centurions"), who were huge favourites in a playoff semi final. In the final, they were comprehensively beaten by [Featherstone Rovers](/wiki/Featherstone_Rovers "Featherstone Rovers"). Sheffield also put in a bid to be a host city for the [2013 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2013_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2013 Rugby League World Cup"), but their bid was unsuccessful.

### Other Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Other Sports")]

Sheffield has a number of [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") clubs that compete in the [Men's England Hockey League](/wiki/Men%27s_England_Hockey_League "Men's England Hockey League"), the [Women's England Hockey League](/wiki/Women%27s_England_Hockey_League "Women's England Hockey League"), the [North Hockey League](/wiki/North_Hockey_Association "North Hockey Association"), the [Yorkshire Hockey Association League](/wiki/Yorkshire_Hockey_Association "Yorkshire Hockey Association") and the [BUCS leagues](/wiki/British_Universities_and_Colleges_Sport "British Universities and Colleges Sport"). These include [Sheffield Hockey Club](/wiki/Sheffield_Hockey_Club "Sheffield Hockey Club") (partnered with [Sheffield Hallam University](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_University "Sheffield Hallam University")), [Sheffield University Bankers Hockey Club](/wiki/Sheffield_University_Bankers_Hockey_Club "Sheffield University Bankers Hockey Club") and [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield") Hockey Club.[[228]](#cite_note-229)[[229]](#cite_note-230)[[230]](#cite_note-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232)[[232]](#cite_note-233)[[233]](#cite_note-234)

[Sheffield Sharks](/wiki/Sheffield_Sharks "Sheffield Sharks") are a professional basketball team established in 1991 who play in the [Super League Basketball](/wiki/Super_League_Basketball "Super League Basketball") at the [Park Community Arena](/wiki/Park_Community_Arena "Park Community Arena").[[234]](#cite_note-235)

[Sheffield Giants](/wiki/Sheffield_Giants "Sheffield Giants") are an [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team who play in the [BAFA National Leagues](/wiki/BAFA_National_Leagues "BAFA National Leagues") Premier Division, the highest level of British American Football.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/IceSheffield.jpg/250px-IceSheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:IceSheffield.jpg)

iceSheffield

Sheffield is home to the [Sheffield Steelers](/wiki/Sheffield_Steelers "Sheffield Steelers") professional ice hockey team who play out of the 9.300 seater [Sheffield Arena](/wiki/Sheffield_Arena "Sheffield Arena")[[235]](#cite_note-236) and are known as one of the top teams in the UK, regularly selling out the arena. They have the 28th highest average attendance rating in Europe, and the highest in the UK. They play in the 10 team professional [Elite Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Elite_Ice_Hockey_League "Elite Ice Hockey League"). Sheffield is also home to the semi-professional ice hockey team Sheffield Steeldogs who play in the NIHL.

The Sheffield Ice Hockey Academy also are based in Sheffield, and play out of [IceSheffield](/wiki/IceSheffield "IceSheffield"), competing in the EIHA Junior North Leagues and have had one player, [Liam Kirk](/wiki/Liam_Kirk_(ice_hockey) "Liam Kirk (ice hockey)"), become the first born and trained British player to be drafted into the NHL, when he was drafted in the [NHL entry draft](/wiki/NHL_entry_draft "NHL entry draft") 189th overall in 2018 by the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes"). The [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") was made in Sheffield in 1892. Sheffield is also home to the [Sheffield Steel Rollergirls](/wiki/Sheffield_Steel_Rollergirls "Sheffield Steel Rollergirls"), a roller derby team.

### Facilities and events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Facilities and events")]

[![A Modern sporting facility. The building is roughly rectangular in shape and is quite modern, with a wooden clad design. It is surrounded by greenery and has several tall, white spikes on its roof.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/English_Institute_of_Sport_Sheffield.png/250px-English_Institute_of_Sport_Sheffield.png)](/wiki/File:English_Institute_of_Sport_Sheffield.png)

English Institute of Sport, Sheffield

[![A large sports centre situated in Sheffield City Centre](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Sheffield_Skyline_from_Park_Hill.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Skyline_from_Park_Hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Skyline_from_Park_Hill.jpg)

[Ponds Forge](/wiki/Ponds_Forge "Ponds Forge") (bottom left) with Sheffield City Centre behind and Park Square in the bottom right

Many of Sheffield's sporting facilities were built for the [World Student Games](/wiki/Universiade "Universiade"), which the city hosted in 1991, including Sheffield Arena and the [Ponds Forge](/wiki/Ponds_Forge "Ponds Forge") international diving and swimming complex. Ponds Forge is also the home of Sheffield City Swimming Club, a local swimming club competing in the [Speedo](/wiki/Speedo "Speedo") league. The former [Don Valley International Athletics Stadium](/wiki/Don_Valley_Stadium "Don Valley Stadium"), once the largest athletics stadium in the UK, was also constructed for the Universiade games.[[236]](#cite_note-237)

Following the closure and demolition of Don Valley Stadium in 2013, The Sheffield Olympic Legacy Park was established and constructed on the same site, adjacent to the English Institute for Sport. The park is designed to a collaborative project with input from numerous stakeholders including both universities in Sheffield, the English Institute of Sport Sheffield, the NHS and private medical companies.[[237]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-238) A key part of this collaboration is Sheffield Hallam University's £14 million Advanced Well-being Research Centre (AWRC), which was established along similar lines to the University of Sheffield's [Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre](/wiki/Advanced_Manufacturing_Park "Advanced Manufacturing Park")'s (AMRC's).[[237]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-238) The site also includes teaching facilities, a stadium and research & innovation facilities.[[237]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-238)

The [Sheffield Ski Village](/wiki/Sheffield_Ski_Village "Sheffield Ski Village") was the largest artificial [ski](/wiki/Ski "Ski") resort in Europe, before being destroyed in a series of suspected arson attacks in 2012 and 2013. The city also has six indoor climbing centres and is home to a significant community of professional climbers, including Britain's most successful competitive climber [Shauna Coxsey](/wiki/Shauna_Coxsey "Shauna Coxsey"). Sheffield was the UK's first National City of Sport and is now home to the [English Institute of Sport – Sheffield](/wiki/English_Institute_of_Sport_%E2%80%93_Sheffield "English Institute of Sport – Sheffield"), where British athletes trained for the 2012 Olympics.[[238]](#cite_note-239)

Sheffield also has close ties with [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker"), with the city's [Crucible Theatre](/wiki/Crucible_Theatre "Crucible Theatre") being the venue for the [World Snooker Championships](/wiki/World_Snooker_Championship "World Snooker Championship").[[239]](#cite_note-240) The English Institute of Sport hosts most of the top fencing competitions each year, including the National Championships for Seniors, Juniors (U20's) and Cadets (U17's) as well as the 2011 Senior European Fencing Championships. The English [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)") open is also held in the city every year. The International Open and World Matchplay Championship [bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls") tournaments have both been held at [Ponds Forge](/wiki/Ponds_Forge "Ponds Forge").[[240]](#cite_note-241) The city also hosts the [Sheffield Tigers](/wiki/Sheffield_Tigers_RUFC "Sheffield Tigers RUFC") rugby union, [Sheffield Sharks](/wiki/Sheffield_Sharks "Sheffield Sharks"), American Football team the [Sheffield Giants](/wiki/Sheffield_Giants "Sheffield Giants"), basketball, [Sheffield University Bankers](/wiki/Sheffield_University_Bankers_Hockey_Club "Sheffield University Bankers Hockey Club") hockey, [Sheffield Steelers](/wiki/Sheffield_Steelers "Sheffield Steelers") ice hockey and [Sheffield Tigers](/wiki/Sheffield_Tigers_(Speedway) "Sheffield Tigers (Speedway)") speedway teams. Sheffield also has many golf courses all around the city.

Sheffield was selected as a candidate host city by the [Football Association (FA)](/wiki/The_FA "The FA") as part of the [England 2018 FIFA World Cup bid](/wiki/England_2018_FIFA_World_Cup_bid "England 2018 FIFA World Cup bid") on 16 December 2009.[[241]](#cite_note-242) [Hillsborough Stadium](/wiki/Hillsborough_Stadium "Hillsborough Stadium") was chosen as the proposed venue for matches in Sheffield.[[242]](#cite_note-243) The bid failed.

Sheffield hosted the finish of Stage 2 of the [2014 Tour de France](/wiki/2014_Tour_de_France "2014 Tour de France"). Within the City limits and located just 4 km (2.5 mi) from the finish, was the ninth and final climb of the stage, the Category 4 Côte de Jenkin Road. The one point in the [King of the Mountains](/wiki/King_of_the_Mountains "King of the Mountains") competition was claimed by [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") of [Team Sky](/wiki/Team_Sky "Team Sky"). The climb was just 0.8 km (0.5 mi) long at an average gradient of 10.8%. The stage was won by the eventual overall winner, [Vincenzo Nibali](/wiki/Vincenzo_Nibali "Vincenzo Nibali") of [Astana Pro Team](/wiki/Astana_Pro_Team "Astana Pro Team").[[243]](#cite_note-244)

[IceSheffield](/wiki/IceSheffield "IceSheffield"), an Ice Rink with 2 [Olympic sized rinks](/wiki/Ice_hockey_rink "Ice hockey rink"), was opened in May 2003, and is home to the Sheffield Steeldogs, Sheffield Ice Hockey Academy, and Sutton Sting amongst other teams. It is the host to the yearly EIHA Conference Tournament, EIHA Nationals, and Sheffield Junior Tournament.

The [Sheffield Half Marathon](/wiki/Sheffield_Half_Marathon "Sheffield Half Marathon") is held annually.[[244]](#cite_note-245) It has thousands of participants every year.[[245]](#cite_note-246)

## Landmarks and parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Landmarks and parks")]

Main article: [List of tourist attractions in Sheffield](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Sheffield "List of tourist attractions in Sheffield")

[Weston Park Museum](/wiki/Weston_Park_Museum "Weston Park Museum") is a museum telling the stories of Sheffield and its people. The museum was originally opened in 1875.[[246]](#cite_note-247)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Weston_Park_Museum.jpg/250px-Weston_Park_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Weston_Park_Museum.jpg)

[Weston Park Museum](/wiki/Weston_Park_Museum "Weston Park Museum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Bishops_House_2011.jpg/250px-Bishops_House_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bishops_House_2011.jpg)

[Bishops' House](/wiki/Bishops%27_House "Bishops' House")

[Bishops' House](/wiki/Bishops%27_House "Bishops' House") was built in 1500 and is a [Grade II listed building](/wiki/Listed_Building "Listed Building") which is now a museum.[[247]](#cite_note-248)

The [Sheffield Walk of Fame](/wiki/Sheffield_Legends "Sheffield Legends") in the City Centre honours famous Sheffield residents past and present in a similar way to the Hollywood version.[[248]](#cite_note-249) Sheffield also had its own Ferris Wheel known as the [Wheel of Sheffield](/wiki/Wheel_of_Sheffield "Wheel of Sheffield"), located atop Fargate shopping precinct. The Wheel was dismantled in October 2010 and moved to London's Hyde Park.[[249]](#cite_note-250)

Heeley City Farm and [Graves Park](/wiki/Graves_Park "Graves Park") are home to Sheffield's two farm animal collections, both of which are fully open to the public.[[250]](#cite_note-251)[[251]](#cite_note-252) Sheffield also has its own zoo; the Tropical Butterfly House, Wildlife & Falconry Centre.[[252]](#cite_note-253)

There are about [1,100 listed buildings in Sheffield](/wiki/Listed_buildings_in_Sheffield "Listed buildings in Sheffield") (including the whole of the [Sheffield postal district](/wiki/S_postcode_area "S postcode area")).[[253]](#cite_note-254) Of these, only five are Grade I listed. Sixty-seven are Grade II\*, but the overwhelming majority are listed as Grade II.[[254]](#cite_note-255) Compared to other English cities, Sheffield has few buildings with the highest Grade I listing: [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), for example, has [26 Grade I listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_buildings_in_Liverpool "Listed buildings in Liverpool"). This situation led the noted architecture historian [Nikolaus Pevsner](/wiki/Nikolaus_Pevsner "Nikolaus Pevsner"), writing in 1959, to comment that the city was "architecturally a miserable disappointment", with no pre-19th-century buildings of any distinction.[[255]](#cite_note-256) By contrast, in November 2007, Sheffield's Peace and Winter Gardens beat London's South Bank to gain the [Royal Institute of British Architects](/wiki/Royal_Institute_of_British_Architects "Royal Institute of British Architects")' Academy of Urbanism "Great Place" Award, as an "outstanding example of how cities can be improved, to make urban spaces as attractive and accessible as possible".[[256]](#cite_note-257) In the summer of 2016 a public art event across the city occurred called the [Herd of Sheffield](/wiki/Herd_of_Sheffield "Herd of Sheffield") which raised £410,000 for the [Sheffield Children's Hospital](/wiki/Sheffield_Children%27s_Hospital "Sheffield Children's Hospital").[[257]](#cite_note-herd-258)

### Greenspace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Greenspace")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Sheffield_Botanical_Gardens.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Botanical_Gardens.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Botanical_Gardens.jpg)

[Sheffield Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Sheffield_Botanical_Gardens "Sheffield Botanical Gardens")

Sheffield has a reputed 4.5 million trees[[7]](#cite_note-GEO_treecount-8) and is considered to be one of the greenest cities in England and the UK.[[258]](#cite_note-259)[[82]](#cite_note-The_four_best_UK_cities_for_green_space-83) There are many parks and woods throughout the city and beyond. Containing more than 250 parks, woodlands and gardens, there are around 78 public parks and 10 public gardens in Sheffield,[[5]](#cite_note-sheffgeog-6) including 83 managed parks (13 'City' Parks, 20 'District' Parks and 50 'Local' Parks) located throughout the city.[[259]](#cite_note-About_the_Parks_&_Countryside_Service-260) Included in the city parks category are 3 of Sheffield's 6 public gardens (the [Sheffield Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Sheffield_Botanical_Gardens "Sheffield Botanical Gardens"), the [Peace Gardens](/wiki/Peace_Gardens "Peace Gardens") and Hillsborough Walled Gardens, with the [Sheffield Winter Gardens](/wiki/Sheffield_Winter_Gardens "Sheffield Winter Gardens"), [Beauchief Gardens](/wiki/Beauchief_Gardens "Beauchief Gardens") and Lynwood Gardens being the separate entities).

The Sheffield Botanical Gardens are on a 19-acre site located to the south-west of the city centre and date back to 1836. The site includes a large, Grade II listed, Victorian era [glasshouse](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse"). The Peace Gardens, neighboured next to the [Town Hall](/wiki/Sheffield_Town_Hall "Sheffield Town Hall") and forming part of the Heart of the City project, occupy a 0.67 hectares (1.7 acres) site in the centre of the city. The site is dominated by its water features, principal among which is the Goodwin Fountain. Made up of 89 individual jets of water, this fountain lies at the corner of the quarter-circle shaped Peace Gardens and is named after [Stuart Goodwin](/wiki/Stuart_Goodwin "Stuart Goodwin"), a notable Sheffield industrialist. Since their redevelopment in 1998, the Peace gardens have received a number of regional and national accolades.[[260]](#cite_note-261) Hillsborough Walled Garden is located in [Hillsborough Park](/wiki/Hillsborough_Park "Hillsborough Park"), to the north-west of the city centre. The gardens date back to 1779 and have been dedicated to the victims of the [Hillsborough Disaster](/wiki/Hillsborough_Disaster "Hillsborough Disaster") since the redevelopment of the gardens in the early 1990s.[[261]](#cite_note-262) [The Winter Garden](/wiki/Sheffield_Winter_Garden "Sheffield Winter Garden"), lying within the [Heart of the City](/wiki/Heart_of_the_City_(development) "Heart of the City (development)"), is a large wood framed, glass skinned [greenhouse](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse") housing some 2,500 plants from around the world.[[262]](#cite_note-263)

Also within the city there are a number of [nature reserves](/wiki/Nature_reserves "Nature reserves") which when combined occupy 1,600 acres (6.5 km2) of land.[[263]](#cite_note-sheffield1-264) There are also 170 [woodland](/wiki/Woodland "Woodland") areas within the city, 80 of which are classed as ancient.[[263]](#cite_note-sheffield1-264)

The south-west boundary of the city overlaps with the [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District"), the first national park in England (est. 1951).[[264]](#cite_note-265) As a consequence, several communities actually reside within both entities. The Peak District is home to many notable, natural, features and also human-made features such as [Chatsworth House](/wiki/Chatsworth_House "Chatsworth House"), the setting for the BBC series *[Pride and Prejudice](/wiki/Pride_and_Prejudice_(1995_TV_series) "Pride and Prejudice (1995 TV series)")*.[[265]](#cite_note-266)

[Sheffield City Council](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council "Sheffield City Council") has created a new chain of parks spanning the hillside behind [Sheffield Station](/wiki/Sheffield_Station "Sheffield Station"). The park, known as Sheaf Valley Park, has an open-air amphitheatre and will include an arboretum.[[266]](#cite_note-sheffieldtelegraph.co.uk-267) The site was once home to a medieval deer park, latterly owned by the [Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk_Heritage_Park "Norfolk Heritage Park").[[266]](#cite_note-sheffieldtelegraph.co.uk-267)

### Theatres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Theatres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Sheffield_Theatres.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Theatres.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Theatres.jpg)

The [Crucible Theatre](/wiki/Crucible_Theatre "Crucible Theatre") (centre) and Lyceum Theatre (right)

Sheffield has two large theatres, the [Lyceum Theatre](/wiki/Lyceum_Theatre_(Sheffield) "Lyceum Theatre (Sheffield)") and the [Crucible Theatre](/wiki/Crucible_Theatre "Crucible Theatre"), which together with the smaller [Studio Theatre](/wiki/Studio_Theatre_(Sheffield) "Studio Theatre (Sheffield)") make up the largest theatre complex outside London, located in [Tudor Square](/wiki/Tudor_Square "Tudor Square").[[267]](#cite_note-268) The Crucible Theatre, a grade II listed building, is the home (since 1977) of the [World Snooker Championships](/wiki/World_Snooker_Championships "World Snooker Championships"), which sees most of Tudor Square and the adjoining Winter Garden used for side events, and hosts many well-known stage productions throughout the year from local, national and international performance groups. The theatre was awarded the Barclays 'Theatre of the Year Award' in 2001. Between 2007 and 2009, the theatre underwent a £15 million refurbishment during which time major internal and external improvements were carried out. The Lyceum, which opened in 1897, serves as a venue for touring [West End productions](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") and operas by [Opera North](/wiki/Opera_North "Opera North"), as well as locally produced shows. Sheffield also has the Montgomery Theatre, a small 420 seater theatre located a short distance from Tudor Square, opposite the town hall on Surrey Street.[[268]](#cite_note-269) There are also a large number of smaller amateur theatres scattered throughout the city.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Sheffield](/wiki/Culture_of_Sheffield "Culture of Sheffield")

Sheffield made the shortlist for the first city to be designated [UK City of Culture](/wiki/UK_City_of_Culture "UK City of Culture"), but in July 2010 it was announced that [Derry](/wiki/Derry "Derry") had been selected.[[269]](#cite_note-270)

Sheffield has a number of [pubs](/wiki/Pubs "Pubs") throughout the city. West Street, running through the heart of the [West End](/wiki/West_End,_Sheffield "West End, Sheffield") district of the city centre, is home to pubs, bars and clubs and attracts student visitors. Recent additions to the city's leisure scene include [Leopold Square](/wiki/Leopold_Square "Leopold Square"), situated just off the northern end of West Street and [Millennium Square](/wiki/Millennium_Square,_Sheffield "Millennium Square, Sheffield"), which are home to several restaurants offering international cuisine. Aagrah, an Indian restaurant in the square which serves [Kashmiri cuisine](/wiki/Kashmiri_cuisine "Kashmiri cuisine"), was prior to 2014 voted "Best Restaurant Group in the UK" at the [British Curry Awards](/wiki/British_Curry_Awards "British Curry Awards").[[270]](#cite_note-271)

Sheffield was once home to a number of historically important nightclubs in the early dance music scene of the 1980s and 1990s, [Gatecrasher One](/wiki/Gatecrasher "Gatecrasher") was one of the most popular clubs in the North of England until its destruction by fire on 18 June 2007.[[271]](#cite_note-272)

Sheffield has a thriving poetry and spoken word scene: from Wordlife's events across the city to the sustained work of Sheffield Authors; from the vibrant monthly arts night, Verse Matters, at the Moor Theatre Deli to the longstanding work of The Poetry Business, there are always opportunities for new and experienced writers to get writing and to share their work.

The city is home to thirteen [morris dance](/wiki/Morris_dance "Morris dance") teams—thought to be one of the highest concentration of sides in the country. Nearly all forms of the dance are represented in the city, including [Cotswold](/wiki/Cotswold_morris "Cotswold morris") (Five Rivers Morris,[[272]](#cite_note-273) Pecsaetan Morris,[[273]](#cite_note-274) Harthill Morris,[[274]](#cite_note-275) Lord Conyer's Morris Men,[[275]](#cite_note-276) Sheffield City Morris,[[276]](#cite_note-277) William Morris[[277]](#cite_note-278)), [border](/wiki/Border_Morris "Border Morris") (Boggart's Breakfast[[278]](#cite_note-279)), [North West](/wiki/North_West_morris "North West morris") (Yorkshire Chandelier,[[279]](#cite_note-280) Silkstone Greens,[[280]](#cite_note-281) Lizzie Dripping[[281]](#cite_note-282)), [rapper](/wiki/Rapper_dance "Rapper dance") (Sheffield Steel Rapper[[282]](#cite_note-283)) and [Longsword](/wiki/Long_Sword_dance "Long Sword dance").

Sheffield hosts a number of festivals, including the [Festival of Debate](/wiki/Festival_of_Debate "Festival of Debate"), the Grin Up North Sheffield Comedy Festival,[[283]](#cite_note-284) the [Sensoria Music & Film Festival](/wiki/Sensoria_Music_%26_Film_Festival "Sensoria Music & Film Festival") and the [Tramlines Festival](/wiki/Tramlines_Festival "Tramlines Festival").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Music")]

See also: [List of musicians from Sheffield](/wiki/List_of_musicians_from_Sheffield "List of musicians from Sheffield")

The Tramlines Festival was launched as an annual music festival in 2009,[[284]](#cite_note-285) it is held at Hillsborough Park (the main stage) and at venues throughout [Sheffield City Centre](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Centre "Sheffield City Centre"), and features local and national artists.[[285]](#cite_note-tramlines-286)

Musical acts originating in Sheffield include [Joe Cocker](/wiki/Joe_Cocker "Joe Cocker"), [Ace](/wiki/Ace_(band) "Ace (band)"), [Def Leppard](/wiki/Def_Leppard "Def Leppard"), [Paul Carrack](/wiki/Paul_Carrack "Paul Carrack"), [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys"), [Bring Me the Horizon](/wiki/Bring_Me_the_Horizon "Bring Me the Horizon"), [65daysofstatic](/wiki/65daysofstatic "65daysofstatic"), [Rolo Tomassi](/wiki/Rolo_Tomassi "Rolo Tomassi"), [While She Sleeps](/wiki/While_She_Sleeps "While She Sleeps"), [Pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(band) "Pulp (band)") and [Moloko](/wiki/Moloko "Moloko").[[286]](#cite_note-287)[[287]](#cite_note-288)[[288]](#cite_note-289)[[289]](#cite_note-290) The Sheffield band [the Long Blondes](/wiki/The_Long_Blondes "The Long Blondes")[[290]](#cite_note-291) are part of what the *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* dubbed the [New Yorkshire](/wiki/New_Yorkshire "New Yorkshire") musical movement.[[291]](#cite_note-292)

Sheffield has been home to several bands and musicians, with a number of [synth-pop](/wiki/Synth-pop "Synth-pop") and other [electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") bands originating from the city.[[292]](#cite_note-293) These included [the Human League](/wiki/The_Human_League "The Human League"), [Heaven 17](/wiki/Heaven_17 "Heaven 17"), [ABC](/wiki/ABC_(band) "ABC (band)"), [Thompson Twins](/wiki/Thompson_Twins "Thompson Twins") and the more [industrially](/wiki/Industrial_music "Industrial music") inclined [Cabaret Voltaire](/wiki/Cabaret_Voltaire_(band) "Cabaret Voltaire (band)") and [Clock DVA](/wiki/Clock_DVA "Clock DVA"). This electronic tradition has continued: [techno](/wiki/Techno_music "Techno music") label [Warp Records](/wiki/Warp_Records "Warp Records") was a central pillar of the [Yorkshire Bleeps and Bass](/wiki/Yorkshire_Bleeps_and_Bass "Yorkshire Bleeps and Bass") scene of the early 1990s, and has gone on to become one of the UK's oldest and best-loved dance music labels.[[293]](#cite_note-294)[[294]](#cite_note-295) More recently, other popular genres of electronic music such as [bassline house](/wiki/Bassline_house "Bassline house") have originated in the city.[[295]](#cite_note-296)

[![Sheffield City Hall, a Neo-classical design with a large portico and prominent pillars which were damaged when a bomb fell on the adjoining Barkers Pool during the Second World War. It is a grade II* listed building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/City_Hall%2C_Barkers_Pool%2C_Sheffield_%28geograph_2846119%29.jpg/250px-City_Hall%2C_Barkers_Pool%2C_Sheffield_%28geograph_2846119%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Hall,_Barkers_Pool,_Sheffield_(geograph_2846119).jpg)

[Sheffield City Hall](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Hall "Sheffield City Hall"), a Grade II\* listed building

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Sheffield_Arena_2022.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Arena_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Arena_2022.jpg)

[Sheffield Arena](/wiki/Sheffield_Arena "Sheffield Arena")

In 1999 the [National Centre for Popular Music](/wiki/National_Centre_for_Popular_Music "National Centre for Popular Music"), a museum dedicated to the subject of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music"), was opened in the city.[[296]](#cite_note-297) It was not as successful as was hoped, however, and later evolved to become a live music venue; then in February 2005, the unusual steel-covered building became the [students' union](/wiki/Students%27_union "Students' union") for Sheffield Hallam University.[[297]](#cite_note-298) Live music venues in the city include [Leadmill](/wiki/Leadmill "Leadmill"), the [Octagon Centre](/wiki/Octagon_Centre "Octagon Centre"), [Corporation](/wiki/Corporation_(nightclub) "Corporation (nightclub)"), the [City Hall](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Hall "Sheffield City Hall"), the [University of Sheffield Students' Union](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield_Students%27_Union "University of Sheffield Students' Union"), the Studio Theatre at the [Crucible Theatre](/wiki/Crucible_Theatre "Crucible Theatre") and the [O2 Academy Sheffield](/wiki/O2_Academy_Sheffield "O2 Academy Sheffield").[[298]](#cite_note-299)[[299]](#cite_note-300)[[300]](#cite_note-301)[[301]](#cite_note-302)[[302]](#cite_note-303)[[303]](#cite_note-304)

The city is home to several orchestras and choirs, such as the Sheffield Symphony Orchestra,[[304]](#cite_note-305) the Sheffield Philharmonic Orchestra,[[305]](#cite_note-306) the [Sheffield Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Sheffield_Chamber_Orchestra "Sheffield Chamber Orchestra"), the City of Sheffield Youth Orchestra, the [Sheffield Philharmonic Chorus](/wiki/Sheffield_Philharmonic_Chorus "Sheffield Philharmonic Chorus") and the [Chorus UK](/wiki/Chorus_UK "Chorus UK") community choir.[[306]](#cite_note-307)[[307]](#cite_note-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309)[[309]](#cite_note-310) It is also home to [Music in the Round](/wiki/Music_in_the_Round "Music in the Round"), a charitable organisation that exists to promote chamber music.

Sheffield has a folk music, song and dance community. Singing and music sessions occur weekly in pubs around the city and it also hosts the annual Sheffield Sessions Festival.[[310]](#cite_note-311) The University of Sheffield runs a number of courses and research projects dedicated to folk culture.[[311]](#cite_note-312)

The tradition of singing carols in pubs around Christmas is still kept alive in the city. The Sheffield Carols, as they are known locally, predate modern carols by over a century and are sung with alternative words and verses.[[312]](#cite_note-313) Although there is a core of carols that are sung at most venues, each particular place has its own mini-tradition. The repertoire at two nearby places can vary widely, and woe betide those who try to strike up a 'foreign' carol. Some are unaccompanied, some have a piano or organ, there is a flip chart with the words on in one place, a string quartet (quintet, sextet, septet) accompanies the singing at another, some encourage soloists, others stick to audience participation, a brass band plays at certain events, the choir takes the lead at another.[[313]](#cite_note-314)

Sheffield was shortlisted to host the [2023 Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2023 "Eurovision Song Contest 2023"),[[314]](#cite_note-315) which took place between 9 and 13 May 2023 in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool").[[315]](#cite_note-316)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/BBC_Radio_Sheffield%2C_Shoreham_Street_%28geograph_5844248%29.jpg/250px-BBC_Radio_Sheffield%2C_Shoreham_Street_%28geograph_5844248%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BBC_Radio_Sheffield,_Shoreham_Street_(geograph_5844248).jpg)

BBC Radio Sheffield

Sheffield has two commercial newspapers, *[The Star](/wiki/Sheffield_Star "Sheffield Star")* and *[Sheffield Telegraph](/wiki/Sheffield_Telegraph "Sheffield Telegraph")*, both published by [JPIMedia](/wiki/JPIMedia "JPIMedia"), which took over the assets of [Johnston Press PLC](/wiki/Johnston_Press "Johnston Press"). *The Star* has been published daily since 1897; the *Sheffield Telegraph*, now a weekly publication, originated in 1855.[[316]](#cite_note-317)

Sheffield has its own TV station; [Sheffield Live TV](/wiki/Sheffield_Live! "Sheffield Live!"), a not-for-profit company which began broadcasting on 23 September 2014.[[317]](#cite_note-318) SLTV has been awarded a 12-year licence to provide the digital terrestrial broadcasting service.[[318]](#cite_note-319)[[319]](#cite_note-320)[[320]](#cite_note-321) Regional broadcasters [BBC Yorkshire](/wiki/BBC_Yorkshire "BBC Yorkshire") and [Yorkshire Television](/wiki/Yorkshire_Television "Yorkshire Television") also cover the city.

Local radio stations broadcasting in the city include [BBC Radio Sheffield](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Sheffield "BBC Radio Sheffield") and [Bauer Group](/wiki/Bauer_Media_Audio_UK "Bauer Media Audio UK") stations [Hits Radio South Yorkshire](/wiki/Hits_Radio_South_Yorkshire "Hits Radio South Yorkshire") (formerly [Hallam FM](/wiki/Hallam_FM "Hallam FM")) & [Greatest Hits Radio South Yorkshire](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_South_Yorkshire "Greatest Hits Radio South Yorkshire"). Sheffield is also home to two [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") licensed [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") stations: [Sheffield Live](/wiki/Sheffield_Live "Sheffield Live"), and Link FM.[[321]](#cite_note-322) [Kiveton Park](/wiki/Kiveton_Park "Kiveton Park")-based community station [Redroad FM](/wiki/Redroad_FM "Redroad FM") also broadcasts to the east of the city.[[322]](#cite_note-323)

Sheffield Hospital Radio broadcasts a 24-hour service to the Royal Hallamshire, Northern General and Weston Park hospitals and also offers a dedicated patient visiting service. The charity is operated by volunteers from studios at the Royal Hallamshire Hospital.[[323]](#cite_note-324)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Film")]

The films and plays *[The Full Monty](/wiki/The_Full_Monty "The Full Monty")*, *[Threads](/wiki/Threads_(1984_film) "Threads (1984 film)")*, *[Looks and Smiles](/wiki/Looks_and_Smiles_(film) "Looks and Smiles (film)")*, *[When Saturday Comes](/wiki/When_Saturday_Comes_(film) "When Saturday Comes (film)")*, *[Whatever Happened to Harold Smith?](/wiki/Whatever_Happened_to_Harold_Smith%3F "Whatever Happened to Harold Smith?")*, *[The History Boys](/wiki/The_History_Boys "The History Boys")* and *[Four Lions](/wiki/Four_Lions "Four Lions")* are set in the city.[[324]](#cite_note-325) *[F.I.S.T.](/wiki/F.I.S.T._(film) "F.I.S.T. (film)")*, *[Kill List](/wiki/Kill_List "Kill List")*, *['71](/wiki/%2771_(film) "'71 (film)")* and *[The Princess Bride](/wiki/The_Princess_Bride_(film) "The Princess Bride (film)")* also include several scenes filmed in Sheffield and a substantial part of *[Among Giants](/wiki/Among_Giants "Among Giants")*[[325]](#cite_note-326) was filmed in the city. The documentary festival [Sheffield Doc/Fest](/wiki/Sheffield_Doc/Fest "Sheffield Doc/Fest") has been run annually since 1994 at the [Showroom Cinema](/wiki/Showroom_Cinema "Showroom Cinema"),[[326]](#cite_note-327) and in 2007 Sheffield hosted the [Awards of the International Indian Film Academy](/wiki/Awards_of_the_International_Indian_Film_Academy "Awards of the International Indian Film Academy").[[327]](#cite_note-328) The 2018 series of *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")*, which features the [Park Hill](/wiki/Park_Hill,_Sheffield "Park Hill, Sheffield") estate and other Sheffield locations, premiered in Sheffield.[[328]](#cite_note-329) A follow-up [series of the same name](/wiki/The_Full_Monty_(TV_series) "The Full Monty (TV series)"), to the 1997 film *The Full Monty*, which released on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") in 2023 was filmed in Sheffield[[329]](#cite_note-330) and Manchester[[330]](#cite_note-331) between 2022 and 2023. Furthermore, scenes in [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") miniseries *[The Regime](/wiki/The_Regime_(miniseries) "The Regime (miniseries)"),* starring [Kate Winslet](/wiki/Kate_Winslet "Kate Winslet") and [Hugh Grant](/wiki/Hugh_Grant "Hugh Grant"), were filmed in Sheffield as well as in [Wentworth Woodhouse](/wiki/Wentworth_Woodhouse "Wentworth Woodhouse"), a stately home in neighbouring [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham").[[331]](#cite_note-332)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Valley_Centertainment%2C_Sheffield.jpg/250px-Valley_Centertainment%2C_Sheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valley_Centertainment,_Sheffield.jpg)

[Valley Centertainment](/wiki/Valley_Centertainment "Valley Centertainment"), Sheffield

[Valley Centertainment](/wiki/Valley_Centertainment "Valley Centertainment"), located in the [Don Valley](/wiki/Lower_Don_Valley "Lower Don Valley"), is the main out of town leisure complex in Sheffield. It opened in the 1990s and was built on land previously occupied by [steel mills](/wiki/Steel_mills "Steel mills") across the road from what is now Sheffield Arena. It is anchored by a 20 screen [Cineworld](/wiki/Cineworld "Cineworld") complex which is the largest in the chain and contains the only [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") screens and [4DX](/wiki/4DX "4DX") screen in Sheffield.[[332]](#cite_note-333) Other features of the complex include a [bowling alley](/wiki/Bowling_alley "Bowling alley"), several chain restaurants, an indoor play area as well as indoor [laser tag](/wiki/Laser_tag "Laser tag"). Sheffield has six other cinema complexes, five of which are in the city centre and a one at Meadowhall—[Odeon Sheffield](/wiki/Odeon_Sheffield "Odeon Sheffield"), situated on Arundel Gate in the city centre, [Curzon](/wiki/Curzon_Cinemas "Curzon Cinemas"), which opened in 2015 in the former Sheffield Banking Company building on George Street,[[333]](#cite_note-334)[[334]](#cite_note-335) [The Light](/wiki/The_Light_Cinemas "The Light Cinemas"), located on The Moor and opened in 2017 as part of the regeneration project, and [Vue](/wiki/Vue_(cinema) "Vue (cinema)"), located within Meadowhall Shopping Centre, are the three other mainstream cinemas in the city. The [Showroom](/wiki/Showroom_Cinema,_Sheffield "Showroom Cinema, Sheffield"), an independent cinema showing non-mainstream productions, is located in [Sheaf Square](/wiki/Sheaf_Square "Sheaf Square"), close to [Sheffield station](/wiki/Sheffield_station "Sheffield station"). In 2002 the Showroom was voted as the best Independent cinema in the country by *Guardian* readers.[[335]](#cite_note-336)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Notable people")]

See also: [List of people from Sheffield](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Sheffield "List of people from Sheffield")

Some of the more notable people from Sheffield include:

* [Sean Bean](/wiki/Sean_Bean "Sean Bean") (Born 1959) – Actor[[336]](#cite_note-CW-337)

## Public services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Public services")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Royal_Hallamshire_Hospital_and_%27B%27_Road%2C_viewed_from_Glossop_Road%2C_Broomhill%2C_Sheffield_-_geograph-3813556.jpg/250px-Royal_Hallamshire_Hospital_and_%27B%27_Road%2C_viewed_from_Glossop_Road%2C_Broomhill%2C_Sheffield_-_geograph-3813556.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Hallamshire_Hospital_and_%27B%27_Road,_viewed_from_Glossop_Road,_Broomhill,_Sheffield_-_geograph-3813556.jpg)

The [Royal Hallamshire Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Hallamshire_Hospital "Royal Hallamshire Hospital")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Sheffield_Graves_Art_Gallery_%26_Central_Library.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Graves_Art_Gallery_%26_Central_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Graves_Art_Gallery_%26_Central_Library.jpg)

[Sheffield Central Library](/wiki/Sheffield_Central_Library "Sheffield Central Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/South_Yorkshire_Fire_and_Rescue_Service_HQ.png/250px-South_Yorkshire_Fire_and_Rescue_Service_HQ.png)](/wiki/File:South_Yorkshire_Fire_and_Rescue_Service_HQ.png)

[South Yorkshire Fire & Rescue](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Fire_and_Rescue "South Yorkshire Fire and Rescue") HQ

Sheffield is policed by [South Yorkshire Police](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Police "South Yorkshire Police") (a [territorial police force](/wiki/Territorial_police_force "Territorial police force")) whose headquarters are in the city. Sheffield constitutes one of its four District commands (Barnsley, Doncaster and Rotherham being the other three).[[345]](#cite_note-346) The force polices an area of approximately 600 sq mi (1,554 km2) and is the 13th-largest force in [England, Wales and Northern Ireland](/wiki/Police_forces_in_the_United_Kingdom "Police forces in the United Kingdom"). Oversight of South Yorkshire Police is conducted by the Police and Crime Commissioner, [Alan Billings](/wiki/Alan_Billings "Alan Billings").

Medical services in Sheffield are provided by three NHS Foundation Trusts:

* [Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust](/wiki/Sheffield_Teaching_Hospitals_NHS_Foundation_Trust "Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust")
* [Sheffield Children's NHS Foundation Trust](/wiki/Sheffield_Children%27s_NHS_Foundation_Trust "Sheffield Children's NHS Foundation Trust")
* Sheffield Health and Social Care NHS Foundation Trust

Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust provides healthcare to people (primarily adults) throughout Sheffield and South Yorkshire. The trusts title includes the word 'teaching' because it undertakes training of medical students at the University of Sheffield and has strong links to Sheffield Hallam University as well. The trust has two campuses: The West Campus containing the [Royal Hallamshire Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Hallamshire_Hospital "Royal Hallamshire Hospital"), the Jessop Wing (maternity wing), [Weston Park Hospital](/wiki/Weston_Park_Hospital "Weston Park Hospital") (specialist cancer treatment) and [Charles Clifford Dental Hospital](/wiki/Charles_Clifford_Dental_Hospital "Charles Clifford Dental Hospital"). The [Northern General Hospital](/wiki/Northern_General_Hospital "Northern General Hospital") is the second 'campus' and is a large facility in the northern suburbs of Sheffield, containing the city's A&E department. Sheffield Children's NHS Foundation Trust provides healthcare for children within the city of Sheffield, South Yorkshire and the UK as a whole.[[346]](#cite_note-347) On 12 June 2020, 66 confirmed deaths caused by the [coronavirus](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") were reported in the [Crabtree and Fir Vale district](/wiki/Burngreave_(ward)#Fir_Vale "Burngreave (ward)") in the three months up to May 2020. The [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") said this was the highest number of coronavirus deaths of any area of England and Wales.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[347]](#cite_note-348)

Sheffield Health and Social Care NHS Foundation Trust provides mental health services, services for people with learning disabilities, substance misuse services, long term neurological conditions, as well as a consortium of GP practises.[[348]](#cite_note-349) The Sheffield Institute for Motor Neurone Disease (also known as Sheffield Institute for Translational Neuroscience – SITraN) has been developed by the University of Sheffield.[[349]](#cite_note-350)

Ambulances are provided by the [Yorkshire Ambulance Service](/wiki/Yorkshire_Ambulance_Service "Yorkshire Ambulance Service"), which itself is an NHS trust.[[350]](#cite_note-351) Fire services in Sheffield are provided by [South Yorkshire Fire and Rescue Service](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Fire_and_Rescue_Service "South Yorkshire Fire and Rescue Service"). For the purposes of fire-fighting and rescue, Sheffield is divided into East and West sub-divisions.[[351]](#cite_note-352)

[Sheffield City Council](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council "Sheffield City Council") is responsible for running fifteen libraries within the city and the Hospital Library Service at Weston Park Hospital. A further sixteen are run by community and volunteer groups with over 1,000 volunteers trained by Sheffield City Council.[[352]](#cite_note-353)[[353]](#cite_note-354) The largest is [Sheffield Central Library](/wiki/Sheffield_Central_Library "Sheffield Central Library") which is collocated with [Graves Art Gallery](/wiki/Graves_Art_Gallery "Graves Art Gallery") on Surrey Street, in [Sheffield City Centre](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Centre "Sheffield City Centre"). The Sheffield Central Library also contains the Local Studies Library with 30,000 items related to local history.

Domestic waste services in Sheffield are provided by [Veolia Environmental Services](/wiki/Veolia#Veolia_Environmental_Services "Veolia") under contract from and on behalf of the council. Council owned/run buildings are maintained by [Kier Group Sheffield](/wiki/Kier_Group "Kier Group") in partnership with the council.[[354]](#cite_note-355)[[355]](#cite_note-356)

Sheffield has a District Energy system that exploits the city's domestic waste, by [incinerating](/wiki/Sheffield_Incinerator "Sheffield Incinerator") it and converting the energy from it to electricity. It also provides hot water, which is distributed through over 25 mi (40 km) of pipes under the city, via two networks. These networks supply heat and hot water for many buildings throughout the city. These include not only cinemas, hospitals, shops and offices, but also universities (Sheffield Hallam University and the University of Sheffield), and residential properties.[[356]](#cite_note-357) Energy generated in a waste plant produces 60 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") of thermal energy and up to 19 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") of electrical energy from 225,000 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonnes "Tonnes") of waste.[[357]](#cite_note-358)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: International relations")]

The Sheffield International Linking Committee promotes Sheffield overseas, especially with six [sister cities](/wiki/Town_twinning "Town twinning"):[[358]](#cite_note-359)

* [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu"), Sichuan, China
* [Anshan](/wiki/Anshan "Anshan"), Liaoning, China
* [Bochum](/wiki/Bochum "Bochum"), North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany
* [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk"), Donetsk Oblast, Ukraine
* [Estelí](/wiki/Estel%C3%AD "Estelí"), Estelí Department, Nicaragua
* [Khmelnytskyi](/wiki/Khmelnytskyi "Khmelnytskyi"), Khmelnytskyi Oblast, Ukraine[[359]](#cite_note-360)

A further four cities have a Friendship Agreement with Sheffield:

* [Kawasaki, Kanagawa](/wiki/Kawasaki,_Kanagawa "Kawasaki, Kanagawa"), Japan
* [Kitwe](/wiki/Kitwe "Kitwe"), Copperbelt Province, Zambia
* [Kotli](/wiki/Kotli "Kotli"), Pakistan-administered Kashmir
* [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), Pennsylvania, United States – This is mainly due to both cities' link with the manufacturing of [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel"), with both cities being known as "[Steel City](/wiki/Steel_City "Steel City")".

Two roads in Sheffield have been named after sister cities; a section of the A6102 in Norton is named Bochum Parkway; and a road in [Hackenthorpe](/wiki/Hackenthorpe "Hackenthorpe") is named Donetsk Way. Likewise in Bochum, Germany, there is a major road called the Sheffield-Ring.

19. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** The word *Wales* derives from the Germanic word *[Walhaz](/wiki/Walhaz "Walhaz")*, and was originally used by the Anglo-Saxons to refer to the native Britons. In reference to the villages of Wales and Waleswood, [S.O. Addy](/wiki/Sidney_Oldall_Addy "Sidney Oldall Addy"), in his *A Glossary of Words Used in the Neighbourhood of Sheffield*, p. 274, states "The Anglo-Saxon invaders or settlers called the old inhabitants or aborigines of this country wealas, or foreigners." See also, "Welsh" in Simpson, Jacqueline; Roud, Stephen (1989). *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*. [Clarendon Press](/wiki/Clarendon_Press "Clarendon Press"). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-19-210019-X](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-19-210019-X "Special:BookSources/0-19-210019-X").
20. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** In an entry dated 827, the *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* states "Egbert led an army against the Northumbrians as far as Dore, where they met him, and offered terms of obedience and subjection, on the acceptance of which they returned home" ([transcription](http://mcllibrary.org/Anglo/part2.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20180413124643/http://mcllibrary.org/Anglo/part2.html) 13 April 2018 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")). Most sources (for example Vickers, *Old Sheffield Town*) state that the date given in the chronicle is incorrect, and that 829 is the more likely date for this event.

124. **[^](#cite_ref-125)** There are numerous sources showing the international reputation of Sheffield for metallurgy, and in particular steel and cutlery manufacture. Some examples are: the [Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary"), which begins its entry for *Sheffield*, "The name of a manufacturing city of Yorkshire, famous for cutlery"; and the *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*, which in its entry for "Sheffield" states that by 1830 Sheffield had earned "recognition as the world centre of high-grade steel manufacture". [David Hey](/wiki/David_Hey "David Hey") in the preface to his 1997 book *Mesters to Masters: A History of the Company of Cutlers in Hallamshire.* ([Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press"), [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-19-828997-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-19-828997-9 "Special:BookSources/0-19-828997-9")) states "It (Sheffield) was known for its cutlery wares long before the incorporation of the [Cutlers' Company](/wiki/Company_of_Cutlers_in_Hallamshire "Company of Cutlers in Hallamshire") in 1624, and long before it acquired an international reputation as the steel capital of the world."
